1995.assessment:
Public debt ~52% of GDP. Fujimori's Brady Plan restructuring (1997) still
being finalized; legacy debt from hyperinflation era and previous governments.
Fiscal deficit near balance as Fujimori implemented structural adjustment.
Primary surplus achieved by mid-1990s. High debt level but trajectory improving
rapidly. IMF program compliance driving fiscal consolidation.

sources.citation: IMF Government Finance Statistics; World Bank WDI

1996.assessment:
Public debt ~47.5% of GDP. Rapid decline from 1995. Brady Plan restructuring
completed; debt relief obtained. Fiscal surplus achieved. Privatization proceeds
reducing financing needs. Debt trajectory strongly downward.

1997.assessment:
Public debt ~42% of GDP. Continued rapid decline. Strong fiscal management
under Fujimori's economic team. Privatization revenues financing debt reduction.
Peru achieving fiscal space for the first time in decades.

1998.assessment:
Public debt ~44% of GDP. Slight increase due to El Nino and Asian crisis
effects on revenues. Government borrowed to fund recovery spending. Temporary
setback in debt reduction trajectory. Fiscal deficit widened modestly.

1999.assessment:
Public debt ~47% of GDP. GDP contraction increased debt ratio. Deficit
widened with lower revenues and economic support spending. Still manageable
by historical standards. Political uncertainty added to fiscal pressures.

2000.assessment:
Public debt ~46% of GDP. Modest improvement. Fiscal policy continuity through
Fujimori collapse. Paniagua transitional government maintained fiscal discipline.
No emergency spending binge. Debt still elevated by later standards.

2001.assessment:
Public debt ~45% of GDP. Toledo government maintained fiscal discipline
despite weak growth. PPK as Finance Minister committed to IMF-style orthodoxy.
Deficit contained. Beginning of sustained debt reduction through commodity
boom growth.

2002.assessment:
Public debt ~46% of GDP. Slight uptick; modest fiscal deficit financing.
Growth accelerating but revenues lagging. Decentralization reform (2002)
created fiscal management challenges. Trend will reverse as commodity boom
revenue surge.

2003.assessment:
Public debt ~47% of GDP. Near peak for this era. Revenues still catching
up to growing economy. Primary surplus improving but overall deficit persisted.
Mining revenues beginning to surge. Fiscal Responsibility Law enacted.

2004.assessment:
Public debt ~44% of GDP. Turning point. Mining revenues surging with commodity
boom. Fiscal surplus achieved. Debt beginning rapid sustained decline.
External debt being prepaid. Peru achieving investment grade trajectory.

2005.assessment:
Public debt ~38% of GDP. Rapid reduction. Mining tax revenues, fiscal surplus.
Government prepaying external debt. IMF canceled precautionary arrangement.
Investment grade sought; rating agencies upgrading Peru. Debt trajectory
among best in Latin America.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; IMF Government Finance Statistics

2006.assessment:
Public debt ~33% of GDP. Continued rapid reduction. Fiscal surplus. Garcia
government inherited strong fiscal position; maintained it despite early
social spending commitments. Investment grade rating achieved (S&P BB+ to BBB-).
External debt reduction accelerating.

2007.assessment:
Public debt ~30% of GDP. Crossed below 30% threshold — significant milestone.
Fiscal surplus ~3% of GDP. Commodity revenues at peak. External debt prepaid
substantially. Peru fiscal position among strongest in Latin America.
Savings fund (Fondo de Estabilizacion Fiscal) building reserves.

2008.assessment:
Public debt ~25% of GDP. Among lowest in Peru's modern history. Fiscal surplus
~3.2% of GDP. Sovereign Wealth Fund building. Economic boom at peak. Peru
exemplar of fiscal prudence. Accumulated reserves for upcoming crisis.

2009.assessment:
Public debt ~27% of GDP. Fiscal deficit deployed for crisis stimulus (~$3B
package). Deficit of ~2% of GDP; drew on Fiscal Stabilization Fund. Despite
stimulus, debt only rose modestly — pre-built fiscal buffers allowing
counter-cyclical policy. Exemplary crisis management.

2010.assessment:
Public debt ~24% of GDP. Rapid recovery to near 2008 low. Fiscal surplus
restored with 8.8% growth and commodity revenues. Stabilization fund rebuilt.
Peru fiscal position showcased as emerging market best practice.

2011.assessment:
Public debt ~22% of GDP. Historical low approaching. Fiscal surplus ~1.9%
of GDP. Humala government inheriting very strong fiscal position. Commodity
revenues still high. Peru among least indebted emerging markets.

2012.assessment:
Public debt ~20% of GDP. Record low for modern Peru. Fiscal surplus ~2.3%
of GDP. Pension fund assets building. International reserves exceeding 30%
of GDP. Exemplary fiscal position.

2013.assessment:
Public debt ~20% of GDP. Near absolute minimum. Fiscal surplus moderating
as commodity prices declined. Revenue windfall from super-cycle ending.
Debt position still exemplary by any standard. Fiscal structural reform
still needed to maintain position post-commodity-boom.

2014.assessment:
Public debt ~21% of GDP. Turning point as fiscal position begins modest
deterioration. Commodity revenues declining; deficit appearing. Humala
government increased social spending. Fiscal deficit ~0.3% of GDP; modest
but break from surplus years. Still extremely low debt.

2015.assessment:
Public debt ~23% of GDP. Rising as fiscal deficit widened (~2% of GDP) with
lower commodity revenues. Government borrowing to maintain social spending.
Infrastructure investment maintaining. Still very low debt; ample fiscal space.

2016.assessment:
Public debt ~24% of GDP. Gradual rise. Fiscal deficit ~2.2% of GDP. PPK
government inheriting modest deficit; committed to fiscal consolidation.
PPK's 'Cierre de Brecha' infrastructure program requiring financing.
Still extremely manageable debt level.

2017.assessment:
Public debt ~25% of GDP. El Nino Costero reconstruction spending increased
deficit to ~3.2% of GDP. PPK political crisis limiting budget management
effectiveness. Still well below 30% threshold. Fiscal rules temporarily
suspended for reconstruction.

2018.assessment:
Public debt ~26% of GDP. Slight increase. Vizcarra's fiscal consolidation
target; deficit narrowing toward 2.3% of GDP. Infrastructure investment
through public-private partnerships. Stable fiscal management despite political
instability. Fiscal rules compliance restored.

2019.assessment:
Public debt ~26% of GDP. Stable. Fiscal deficit ~1.5% of GDP. Vizcarra
government maintained discipline through constitutional crisis. Peru's
pre-COVID fiscal position strong; ample headroom for any downturn.
International reserves ~$66B. Excellent fiscal health entering crisis.

2020.assessment:
Public debt ~35% of GDP. COVID-induced surge. Fiscal deficit ~8.9% of GDP —
largest in decades. Government spent ~20% of GDP on COVID response (world
largest stimulus proportional to GDP among emerging markets). Reactiva Peru
(~8% GDP), BonusCovid transfers, health spending. Debt remains manageable
given pre-crisis low base.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI; IMF Peru Article IV

2021.assessment:
Public debt ~36% of GDP. Near peak post-COVID. Fiscal deficit ~2.5% of GDP
as economy rebounded. Castillo election uncertainty; some IMF/rating agency
concerns about policy direction. Still manageable debt. Sol depreciation
added to debt in sol terms.

2022.assessment:
Public debt ~34% of GDP. Declining. Fiscal deficit narrowed to ~1.6% of GDP.
Strong commodity revenues (copper, gold). Castillo crisis (December 2022)
caused short-term fiscal uncertainty but BCRP and Finance Ministry maintained
continuity. Boluarte government committed to fiscal rules compliance.

2023.assessment:
Public debt ~34% of GDP. GDP recession (-0.6%) and lower revenues. Fiscal
deficit widened modestly (~2.1% of GDP). Post-Castillo protest disruptions
reduced mining revenues H1. Boluarte government maintained fiscal discipline.
Debt still manageable.

2024.assessment:
Public debt ~33% of GDP estimated. Growth recovery (~3%) improving revenues.
Deficit narrowing toward ~1.5% of GDP. IMF projecting fiscal consolidation
path. Boluarte government maintaining fiscal rules. Stable fiscal health.

2025.assessment:
Public debt ~32-33% of GDP projected. IMF projecting continued gradual
consolidation. Growth supporting revenues. Pre-election pressures (2026)
may limit consolidation. Deficit ~1-1.5% of GDP. Manageable fiscal position.

1995.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,421 (current USD). Peru recovering from 1980s hyperinflation
and deep recession under Fujimori's stabilization. Economy grew strongly
1993-1995 following structural adjustment. Still far below regional middle-income
peers; poverty high. SL insurgency winding down reducing destruction.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators, GDP per capita (current US$) Peru

1996.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,574. Continued recovery. Fujimori economic team maintained
orthodox macro policies; growth slowed slightly from 1994-1995 boom. Inflation
further declining. Investment growing.

1997.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,758. Strong growth continuing. Peru largely avoided Asian
financial crisis impact in 1997 (though affected 1998-1999). Privatization
proceeds boosting investment. Mining and fishing sectors growing.

1998.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,546. Decline from 1997 peak due to El Nino (1997-1998)
devastating fisheries and agriculture, combined with Asian financial crisis
contagion reducing commodity prices. GDP growth fell to ~0.3% in 1998.

1999.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,273. GDP contracted ~0.4% in real terms; current USD
declined further. Brazil crisis contagion; commodity price collapse. Political
uncertainty around Fujimori third-term bid added to economic headwinds.
Unemployment rising. One of the weakest years of the decade.

2000.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,075. Political crisis (Fujimori fled November 2000) created
uncertainty but economic team maintained continuity. GDP growth ~2.7% real
but currency/USD effects reduced current USD value. Transition period;
investment cautious pending political stabilization.

2001.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,085. Modest recovery beginning under Toledo. Global
recession (9/11 effects) limited growth to ~0.6%. Toledo's economic team
maintained Fujimori-era orthodoxy. Beginning of long commodity cycle expansion.

2003.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,353. Solid growth (~4% real). Copper and gold prices
rising. Toledo government unpopular but macroeconomic fundamentals strong.
FTAs being negotiated. Mining investment pipeline expanding.

2004.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,576. Growth ~5%. Commodity boom accelerating. Mining
sector expanding. Macroeconomic stability (low inflation, fiscal surplus).
Strong investment inflows.

2005.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$2,948. Growth ~6.3%. Major mining projects (Antamina,
Yanacocha) driving exports. Copper price surge. FTA with US signed 2006.
Poverty declining significantly.

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$3,434. Growth ~7.5%. Peru entering golden decade. Garcia
elected; maintained pro-growth policies. Copper price at record high; gold,
zinc also strong. FDI surging into mining. Poverty rate falling rapidly.

2007.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$3,952. Growth ~8.5%. Economic star of Latin America.
Garcia government aggressively promoting mining investment. Commodity prices
near peak. US FTA implemented. Foreign reserves building. Poverty declining.

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$4,699. Growth ~9.8% — highest in decades. Commodity price
peak (copper high, gold $850+/oz). Mining, construction, services all booming.
Global financial crisis hit H2 2008 but Peru insulated by reserves.

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$4,465. Global financial crisis: Peru grew only ~1% real
(one of best in LatAm). Government fiscal stimulus package (~$3B). Recovery
faster than regional peers. Commodity prices recovering by end of year.

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$5,254. Rapid recovery: ~8.8% real growth. Commodity super-cycle
resuming. China demand strong; copper prices surging. New mining projects
online. Peru crossed to upper-middle-income category.

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,067. Growth ~6.3%. Humala election caused brief market
concerns but economy continued strongly. Commodity prices high. Mining
investment record. Approaching decade peak.

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,612. Growth ~6%. Commodity prices beginning to slow.
Mining investment still high but Conga conflict creating uncertainty.
Near historical peak. ~93% of eventual peak.

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,721. Growth ~5.8%. Commodity prices declining from 2011
peaks. Still near historical maximum. Infrastructure investment continuing.
Upper-middle-income country with declining poverty (~23%).

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,567. Growth slowed to ~2.4% — lowest in decade. Commodity
prices declining (copper, gold down). Sol depreciation vs USD reduced current
USD value. Post-boom adjustment.

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,090. Growth ~3.3%. Commodity prices continuing to fall.
Sol depreciation reducing USD value further. El Nino 2015-16 affecting
agriculture. GDP in local currency terms growing faster than USD figure suggests.

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,111. Growth ~3.9%. New mining projects (Las Bambas, Cerro
Verde expansion) boosting copper output. PPK election; business confidence
improved. Still below 2013 peak due to commodity prices and sol depreciation.

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,658. Growth ~2.5% — slowed by El Nino Costero (coastal
flooding, January-March 2017) causing ~$3B damage. Political crisis
(PPK-Fujimorismo) dampened investment. But near 2013 peak.

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,005. Growth ~4%. New historical peak. Vizcarra anti-
corruption reforms did not undermine economy; mining continued strong.
Public infrastructure investment resumed. Peru exceeded 2013 peak in USD terms.

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,115. Growth ~2.2%. Constitutional crisis (Congress dissolved
September 2019) created uncertainty. Trade war headwinds. Peru maintained
growth above regional average. New peak in absolute terms.

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$5,956. COVID catastrophe: GDP contracted ~11.1% — worst
decline in modern history. World's strictest lockdown failed to prevent
highest per-capita death rate. Three presidents in one week (November 2020).
GDP fell to 2013-2014 equivalent levels. Massive fiscal stimulus deployed.

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$6,693. Strong rebound: ~13.3% real growth. Base effect
from 2020 collapse. Copper prices hit record highs (~$10,000/ton). Mining
recovery; services reopening. Below 2018-2019 peak in USD terms due to
sol depreciation.

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,125. Growth ~2.7%. Full recovery to pre-COVID levels.
Castillo self-coup December 2022; protests killing 60+ caused regional
disruption. Mining sector resilient. New approximate peak despite political crisis.

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,033. GDP contracted ~0.6% real (recession). Consecutive
monthly declines H1 2023 due to post-Castillo protest disruptions, mining
blockades, El Nino effects, and global slowdown. Recovery H2 2023.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators; IMF Peru Article IV 2024

2024.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,200 estimated. Recovery from 2023 recession; ~3% real
growth expected. Mining sector resilient. Investment cautious amid Boluarte
political uncertainty. Near previous peak.

2025.assessment:
GDP per capita ~$7,400 projected. IMF projecting ~2.7-3% real growth for
2025. Pre-election year (2026 elections). Mining sector expected to remain
engine of growth. Political uncertainty under Boluarte continuing but economy
showing resilience.

1995.assessment:
Inflation 10.2%. Dramatic improvement from hyperinflation under Garcia first
term (7,649% in 1990). Fujimori's 1990-1992 shock therapy brought inflation
from thousands to double digits by 1993-1995. BCRP (central bank) pursuing
inflation targeting. Downward trajectory toward single digits.

1996.assessment:
Inflation 11.5%. Slight uptick from 1995. BCRP maintaining restrictive monetary
policy. El Nino effects on food prices. Still on downward long-term trajectory.
Convergence toward single digits continuing but gradual.

1997.assessment:
Inflation 8.5%. Broke below double digits — significant milestone. BCRP
inflation targeting framework increasingly effective. Strong economic growth
without inflationary pressure. Structural reforms deepening. Peru achieving
price stability after a decade of chaos.

1998.assessment:
Inflation 7.3%. Continued moderation despite El Nino agricultural disruptions
and Asian financial crisis. BCRP tightening. GDP growth near zero but inflation
continued to fall — testament to monetary policy credibility.

1999.assessment:
Inflation 3.5%. Sharp drop toward price stability. GDP contraction helped
suppress demand-pull inflation. BCRP formally adopted inflation targeting
framework. Peru achieving emerging market price stability benchmarks. Close
to BCRP target range.

2000.assessment:
Inflation 3.8%. Stable near BCRP target (1-3% target band later formalized).
Political crisis (Fujimori collapse) did not destabilize monetary policy.
BCRP independence maintained through political transition. Sol stable.
Inflation well-anchored.

2001.assessment:
Inflation 2.0%. Within BCRP target band. Low growth environment (0.6% real
GDP) suppressed demand pressure. Toledo government maintained monetary
policy continuity. Price stability achieved after a decade of reform.

2002.assessment:
Inflation 0.2%. Near deflation. BCRP accommodative to support growth recovery.
Strong fiscal discipline. Commodity prices not yet spiking. Peru demonstrating
exceptional price stability in a region prone to inflation.

2003.assessment:
Inflation 2.5%. Within BCRP target. Normal functioning economy. Commodity
prices rising but not yet causing inflation. BCRP maintaining price stability
while supporting growth. Toledo government economic performance solid.

2004.assessment:
Inflation 3.7%. Slightly above BCRP target range (1-3%). Global commodity
prices feeding through to domestic prices. Energy and food price pressures.
Still well-controlled by regional standards. BCRP beginning tightening cycle.

2005.assessment:
Inflation 1.6%. Well within target. Strong growth without inflationary pressure.
BCRP credibility fully established. Sol appreciating with commodity boom.
Excellent price stability environment.

2006.assessment:
Inflation 2.0%. Garcia second term began well — no repeat of first-term
(1985-1990) inflationary disaster. BCRP independent and credible. Strong
economic growth of 7.5% without inflation. Commodity boom period with
anchored expectations.

2007.assessment:
Inflation 1.8%. Excellent price stability during 8.5% GDP growth. BCRP
demonstrating that rapid growth and price stability compatible. Commodity
export prices boosting revenues without domestic inflation pass-through.

2008.assessment:
Inflation 5.8%. Global food and energy price shock (2007-2008 commodity price
surge) fed through to consumer prices. BCRP raised rates aggressively.
Above target but controlled by global standards. Peru less affected than
regional neighbors. BCRP credibility limited passthrough.

2009.assessment:
Inflation 2.9%. Returned to target as global commodity shock passed. BCRP
eased to support growth during global financial crisis. Demand moderation
and lower commodity prices brought inflation back down. Within target band.

2010.assessment:
Inflation 1.5%. Strong 8.8% growth with very low inflation. BCRP exemplary
performance. Reserve accumulation reducing currency pressures. Peru showcased
as emerging market monetary policy success story.

2011.assessment:
Inflation 3.4%. Slightly above BCRP target. Global food/commodity price
pressures feeding through. BCRP raised rates. Despite Humala election concerns,
inflation expectations remained anchored. Transitory overshoot.

2012.assessment:
Inflation 3.7%. Above target but manageable. BCRP maintained credibility.
Demand-side pressures from strong growth. Food prices. BCRP incrementally
adjusting monetary stance.

2013.assessment:
Inflation 2.8%. Back within BCRP target (1-3%). Growth slowing reduced
demand pressures. Commodity prices declining. Taper tantrum affecting
sol but BCRP managed. Good inflation performance.

2014.assessment:
Inflation 3.2%. Marginally above target. Sol depreciation vs USD fed into
import prices. Food price increases. BCRP navigating slower growth with
higher inflation. Stagflation concerns modest but passing.

2015.assessment:
Inflation 3.5%. Above target. Sol continued depreciating with commodity
price decline. El Nino affecting food prices. BCRP raised rates despite
slowing growth — credibility preservation. Supply-side shocks driving
overshoot.

2016.assessment:
Inflation 3.6%. Slight overshoot continuing. El Nino 2015-2016 food price
effects. Sol stabilizing. BCRP managing supply-side inflation. PPK government
maintained monetary policy independence. Above target but controlled.

2017.assessment:
Inflation 2.8%. Back within target. El Nino Costero flooding caused temporary
food disruptions H1 2017 but BCRP kept annual inflation in check. Sol
appreciating slightly. BCRP easing as growth slowed from flooding impacts.

2018.assessment:
Inflation 1.3%. Well below midpoint. BCRP accommodative amid 4% growth.
Good supply conditions. Sol stable to appreciating. Vizcarra government
not disrupting monetary stability. Excellent price stability.

2019.assessment:
Inflation 2.1%. Within target. Political crisis (Congress dissolved) did
not affect monetary stability. BCRP independent. Moderate growth (2.2%).
Pre-COVID economic normalcy.

2020.assessment:
Inflation 1.8%. Demand destruction from COVID lockdown (strictest globally)
kept inflation below target midpoint despite massive fiscal/monetary stimulus.
Reactiva Peru guarantees injected ~10% GDP. Three presidents did not destabilize
monetary policy. BCRP cut rates to historic lows.

2021.assessment:
Inflation 6.4%. Global supply chain disruptions and commodity price surge
post-COVID drove inflation above target. Castillo election created currency
uncertainty (sol fell sharply). BCRP began raising rates. Sol depreciation
imported inflation. Above target but manageable by global standards.

2022.assessment:
Inflation 8.0%. Global inflation surge (Ukraine war effects on food/energy).
Highest in ~25 years. BCRP aggressively raised rates (reference rate from
1% to 7.75%). Sol depreciation adding imported inflation. Castillo crisis
Q4 2022 added further uncertainty. Food prices high; poor households hardest hit.

2023.assessment:
Inflation ~6.0%. Declining from 2022 peak as global pressures eased. BCRP
began cutting rates cautiously. Sol stabilizing. Food prices moderating.
Still above target but on downward trajectory. Economic recession (-0.6%)
dampening demand.

2024.assessment:
Inflation ~2.9% estimated. Return to near BCRP target (1-3%). Global inflation
normalization, BCRP rate cuts, stronger sol. Economic recovery supporting
demand without inflationary pressure. Price stability largely restored.

2025.assessment:
Inflation ~2.3% projected. BCRP expected to maintain rates within target.
Pre-election environment; fiscal pressures building but monetary independence
maintained. Price stability well-established institutional framework.

1995.assessment:
Trade openness ~31% of GDP. Peru undergoing trade liberalization under Fujimori's
structural adjustment: tariff reductions, FTA preparations, end of import
substitution policies. Still relatively closed by emerging market standards.
Mining exports (copper, gold, zinc) growing; fish meal; some manufactured
goods. Imports of capital equipment for investment boom.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators, Trade % of GDP Peru

1996.assessment:
Trade openness ~32%. Slight increase. Trade liberalization deepening; tariff
reductions continuing. Mining exports growing. Integration with global commodity
markets advancing.

1997.assessment:
Trade openness ~34%. Growing integration. Mining expansion (Antamina project
begun) boosting export pipeline. Import of capital goods for investment.
Peru signed trade agreements with Andean Community. Pre-Asian crisis open
economy trajectory.

1998.assessment:
Trade openness ~32.5%. Slight decline as El Nino devastated fisheries exports
and Asian crisis reduced commodity prices. Import demand also fell with slower
growth. Temporary reversal in trade share.

1999.assessment:
Trade openness ~32%. Low growth constraining trade volumes. Commodity prices
at cycle low. Import compression as investment slowed. Maintained liberalized
trade framework despite economic difficulties.

2000.assessment:
Trade openness ~33%. Gradual recovery. Commodity export recovery beginning.
Political crisis (Fujimori collapse) did not reverse trade liberalization
framework. Trade policy continuity through Paniagua transition.

2001.assessment:
Trade openness ~31%. Post-9/11 global demand reduction. Slow domestic growth
constraining imports. Toledo maintained open trade stance. US-Peru FTA
negotiations beginning.

2002.assessment:
Trade openness ~33%. Recovery underway. China commodity demand surge benefiting
Peruvian mining exports. Import of capital goods increasing with investment
revival. Commodity super-cycle beginning to raise trade share.

2003.assessment:
Trade openness ~34.5%. Growing. Mining exports expanding. US-Peru FTA
negotiations progressing. Import demand growing with 4% GDP growth.
Trade-to-GDP ratio trending upward.

2004.assessment:
Trade openness ~38%. Strong increase. Commodity price surge driving export
values higher. Copper, gold, zinc export values booming. Import of capital
equipment for mining expansion. Crossing moderate-to-restricted boundary.

2005.assessment:
Trade openness ~41%. Crossed into moderate territory. Antamina, Yanacocha
at production peaks; copper prices high. Gold exports substantial. Import
demand strong with 6.3% growth. FTA with US signed (ratified 2009). Trade
integration accelerating.

2006.assessment:
Trade openness ~47%. Rapid increase. Commodity prices near peak. Copper
at record values; export revenues booming. Investment imports high. Garcia
second term embracing open trade agenda. FTA implementation progressing.

2007.assessment:
Trade openness ~48%. Near peak. Commodity export values high; import demand
booming with 8.5% growth. US FTA ratified. Multiple FTA negotiations underway
(Chile, China, Canada). Peru becoming highly trade-integrated.

2009.assessment:
Trade openness ~42%. Sharp decline from 2008 peak due to commodity price
collapse in global financial crisis. Export values fell sharply (copper price
halved). Import demand also fell with slower growth. US FTA entered force
February 2009. Underlying trade integration intact.

2010.assessment:
Trade openness ~47%. Recovery with commodity prices and strong 8.8% growth.
China FTA entered force March 2010. Mining exports recovering; import demand
strong. China-Peru trade relationship growing significantly.

2011.assessment:
Trade openness ~50%. Commodity prices near peak. Multiple FTAs in force
(US, China, Chile, Canada, South Korea). Peru highly integrated globally.
Humala government maintained open trade policies despite nationalist rhetoric.

2012.assessment:
Trade openness ~47%. Slight decline as commodity prices softened. Still
highly integrated. FTA network expanding; Japan FTA entered force 2012.
China increasingly dominant trading partner. Import demand strong with 6% growth.

2013.assessment:
Trade openness ~44%. Continuing decline in trade ratio as commodity prices
fell from 2011 peaks. Gold, copper prices declined. Export values down.
Import demand also moderating with slower growth (5.8%). FTA network intact.

2014.assessment:
Trade openness ~42%. Post-commodity-boom adjustment. Export values declining
with lower copper/gold/silver prices. Import demand moderating. Still moderate
openness level with extensive FTA network.

2015.assessment:
Trade openness ~43%. New mining projects (Las Bambas, Cerro Verde expansion)
boosting copper export volumes even as prices fell. GDP denominator also
affected by sol depreciation. Overall openness stable.

2016.assessment:
Trade openness ~44%. Stable. Las Bambas copper mine operational; large
contribution to exports. Commodity prices recovering somewhat. PPK government
committed to trade openness and FTA expansion. Trans-Pacific Partnership
negotiations included Peru.

2017.assessment:
Trade openness ~43%. Stable despite El Nino Costero disruption (mainly
domestic). Copper prices recovering. CPTPP (successor to TPP) included Peru.
Trade integration well-established across FTA network.

2018.assessment:
Trade openness ~45%. Increasing. Commodity prices stable to rising. 4% GDP
growth with strong import demand. CPTPP entered force December 2018 with
Peru as member. FTA network: US, EU, China, Japan, Canada, South Korea,
Thailand, Chile, Mexico, Panama, Singapore, Australia, others.

2019.assessment:
Trade openness ~44%. Stable. US-China trade war uncertainty affected commodity
prices but Peru's diversified FTA network provided resilience. Copper prices
volatile. Political crisis (Congress dissolved) not affecting trade policy.

2020.assessment:
Trade openness ~41%. COVID disrupted global trade; import demand collapsed
with 11.1% GDP contraction. Mining continued (classified essential) but
supply chain disruptions. Trade ratio declined but less than GDP suggesting
trade sector relatively resilient vs domestic economy.

2021.assessment:
Trade openness ~48%. Recovery. Copper price hit record highs (~$10,000/ton)
driving export values sharply up. Import demand rebounded with 13.3% GDP
growth. Peru trade sector recovered to near pre-COVID and near commodity
boom levels.

2022.assessment:
Trade openness ~47%. Stable at high level. Commodity prices high (especially
copper, lithium). Import values high partly due to energy/food price inflation.
Castillo crisis limited disruption to trade flows. Mining continued.

2023.assessment:
Trade openness ~44-45%. Declining slightly with lower commodity prices and
GDP recession (-0.6%). Post-Castillo protests causing regional disruption
(Las Bambas access roads blocked) reduced mining export volumes H1 2023.
Recovery H2 2023. FTA network intact.

2024.assessment:
Trade openness ~45% estimated. Recovery from 2023 disruptions. Mining export
volumes recovering; copper prices elevated. Import demand recovering with
~3% growth. FTA network expanding; negotiations ongoing.

2025.assessment:
Trade openness ~45-46% projected. Stable at moderate level. Mining sector
exports continuing to drive trade. FTA network providing diversification.
Pre-election period unlikely to shift trade policy orientation.

1995.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.2%. Peru's labor market characterized by large informal sector
(~70% of employment). Official unemployment understates labor underutilization.
Post-hyperinflation recovery creating formal jobs in Lima. SL insurgency
wind-down reducing rural economic disruption. Economy growing but poverty
still high (~50%+). Lima urban unemployment higher than national figure.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators, Unemployment ILO estimate Peru

1996.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.0%. Stable. Economic growth creating formal employment.
Privatizations completed under Fujimori created some formal sector jobs.
Lima urban area seeing construction and services expansion. Informal sector
still dominant.

1997.assessment:
Unemployment ~7.7%. Declining with strong growth (~6.9%). Formal sector
employment increasing. Mining expansion creating jobs in regions. Lima
construction boom. Downward trend in unemployment.

1998.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.5%. Rising as El Nino and Asian crisis slowed growth to near
zero. Fishing sector jobs lost; construction slowdown. Regional employment
disrupted. Rise moderate given severity of external shocks.

1999.assessment:
Unemployment ~9.4%. GDP contraction (-0.4%) pushing unemployment near double
digits. Brazil/Russia crisis effects. Political uncertainty adding to economic
insecurity. Near-peak unemployment for the 1990s-era stabilization period.
Informal sector absorbing excess labor.

2000.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.5%. GDP grew ~2.7% real providing modest employment gains.
Fujimori collapse (November 2000) created uncertainty but no major employment
shock. Transition period; investment cautious. Slight improvement from 1999.

2001.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.8%. Near-stagnant growth (0.6%) in year of transition.
9/11 effects dampening global demand. Toledo government implementing economic
continuity policies. Employment creation insufficient to absorb labor force
growth.

2002.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.5%. Modest improvement with ~5% growth. Commodity boom
beginning to create mining sector jobs. Construction recovering. Formal
employment in Lima growing. Informal sector still dominant.

2003.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.4%. Slow improvement. 4% growth creating jobs but labor
force growing rapidly. Strikes and social unrest (2003) in some sectors.
Mining jobs in provinces; Lima services recovering.

2004.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.0%. Declining with accelerating growth (~5%). Mining boom
creating provincial employment. Construction expanding. Export sector
(garments, mining) generating formal jobs. Positive trajectory.

2005.assessment:
Unemployment ~7.6%. Continued improvement with 6.3% growth. Mining employment
at record levels. Lima services sector expanding rapidly. Poverty declining
with employment growth. Construction boom.

2006.assessment:
Unemployment ~7.2%. Strong decline with 7.5% growth. Garcia second term
began; maintained growth momentum. Lima formal employment growing. Mining
provincial jobs substantial. Poverty rate falling significantly.

2007.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.8%. Continued improvement. 8.5% GDP growth driving job creation.
Construction boom; mining; services. Lima formal sector growing. Poverty
declining to ~40%. Approaching historical labor market improvement levels.

2008.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.4%. Lowest in many years. Near-10% GDP growth creating
abundant formal employment. Lima services, construction, mining at peak
employment. Global financial crisis impact on employment minimal in 2008
(effects more in 2009).

2009.assessment:
Unemployment ~7.2%. Global financial crisis impact: 1% GDP growth slowed
job creation. Some export sector (garments) jobs lost. Lima urban unemployment
rose. Government fiscal stimulus (Reactiva predecessor programs) cushioned
impact. Still not severe given global crisis magnitude.

2010.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.7%. Rapid recovery with 8.8% GDP growth. Mining, construction,
services all expanding. Lima formal employment growing quickly. Labor market
tightening. Wages beginning to rise in formal sector.

2011.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.3%. Near historical low. Strong 6.3% growth. Humala election
not disrupting employment. Mining sector peak employment. Construction at
record levels. Lima formal employment in services expanding.

2012.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.1%. 6% growth maintaining strong employment. Multiple large
construction projects ongoing. Lima services expanding. Formal sector
employment growing as percentage of total labor force.

2013.assessment:
Unemployment ~5.9%. Approaching historically low levels. Growth slowing
(5.8%) but still strong. Labour market tighter than any point in recent history.
Wages rising in formal sector; poverty at ~23%.

2014.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.0%. Slight uptick as growth slowed to 2.4%. Mining investment
declining; construction slowing. Some formal sector jobs lost. Labor market
softening. Commodity cycle decline impact on employment.

2015.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.3%. Continuing drift upward with sub-4% growth. Post-commodity-
boom adjustment. Lima urban unemployment higher. Some service sector weakness.
Informal absorption of workers released from formal sector.

2016.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.3%. Stable despite 3.9% growth. New mining projects (Las
Bambas) creating provincial employment but not translating to national
unemployment reduction. PPK government beginning infrastructure push.

2017.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.7%. El Nino Costero flooding (January-March 2017) disrupted
northern coastal employment (agriculture, fishing, construction). Growth
slowed to 2.5%. Political crisis also dampening investment and employment.
Some recovery H2 2017.

2018.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.5%. Modest improvement with 4% growth. Vizcarra government
anti-corruption push not impacting employment. Infrastructure investment
resuming. New mining projects online. Lima formal sector growing.

2019.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.4%. Slight improvement. 2.2% growth providing modest employment
gains. Constitutional crisis (Congress dissolved) created uncertainty but
employment relatively resilient. Pre-COVID last normal year.

2020.assessment:
Unemployment ~13.0%. COVID catastrophe: world's strictest lockdown shut down
economy for months. Lima urban unemployment exceeded 20% at peak (May-July 2020).
Millions lost formal sector jobs; many unable to work informally under lockdown.
GDP fell 11.1%. Three presidents in one week added to chaos. Reactiva Peru
loan guarantee program preserved some formal employment.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators; INEI (Peru National Statistics) 2020

2021.assessment:
Unemployment ~8.5%. Rapid improvement with 13.3% GDP rebound. Economy
reopening; services, construction recovering. Some COVID second/third wave
disruptions H1 2021. Still above pre-COVID baseline. Castillo election
uncertainty not significantly impacting employment.

2022.assessment:
Unemployment ~7.0%. Continuing recovery toward pre-COVID levels. Castillo
crisis (December 2022) and protests caused regional disruptions but limited
national employment impact. Mining sector unaffected. Services recovering.
Approaching 2019 baseline.

2023.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.8%. GDP recession (-0.6%) caused slight uptick. Post-Castillo
protests in H1 2023 disrupted regional employment (blockades, mining shutdowns).
Lima formal sector relatively resilient. El Nino effects on agriculture.
Recovery H2 2023 partially offset H1 weakness.

sources.citation: INEI 2023 Labor Market Report; World Bank estimates

2024.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.5% estimated. Recovery with ~3% GDP growth. Labor market
gradually returning to pre-COVID trajectory. Mining sector strong. Services
recovering. Still slightly above 2019 level.

2025.assessment:
Unemployment ~6.3% projected. Further gradual improvement expected with
continued economic recovery. Pre-election year employment dynamics. Mining
and services leading job creation. Returning to pre-COVID structural range.

1995.assessment:
Full international diplomatic recognition. OAS member in good standing.
UN member with normal participation. Bilateral relations with all major
powers (US, EU, China, Japan). Some tension with Ecuador over ongoing border
dispute (Cenepa War January-February 1995). US-Peru relations normal despite
human rights criticism. IMF program ongoing. Active in regional organizations
(Andean Community, SELA, Rio Group). Minor tensions with Ecuadorian conflict
managed diplomatically.

sources.citation: US State Department Background Notes Peru 1995; OAS records

1996.assessment:
Full international integration. Ecuador-Peru peace process underway (Brasilia
Declaration framework). MRTA hostage crisis (December 1996) generated international
attention; Peru received strong international support for anti-terrorism position.
Normal diplomatic relations with all major partners.

1997.assessment:
Full integration with minor tensions. Ivcher case and Constitutional Tribunal
dismissals drew OAS criticism. Some US Congressional concern. MRTA hostage
crisis resolved April 1997 (military assault). Peru's reputation slightly
damaged but no diplomatic isolation. OAS General Assembly discussions about
democratic practices.

1998.assessment:
Full integration. Ecuador-Peru Peace Treaty signed October 1998 (Brasilia
Accord) — end of Cenepa border dispute. Major diplomatic success. US, Brazil,
Argentina, Chile as guarantors. Peru's standing improved with peaceful
resolution of border conflict.

sources.citation: Brasilia Accord October 1998; US State Department 1998

1999.assessment:
Full integration with increasing concern. OAS, US, EU increasingly critical
of Fujimori third-term bid and election manipulation. OAS electoral observation
mission. International community monitoring. Still diplomatically integrated
but facing growing reputational pressure.

sources.citation: OAS Electoral Observation Mission 1999-2000; US State Department 1999

2000.assessment:
Partial isolation during crisis, then rapid reintegration. Post-Vladivideo
OAS strongly critical; special mission sent. US suspended some aid. But
Fujimori's departure and Paniagua transitional government restored relationships
within months. International community actively supported Peru's democratic
transition. End of year: effectively full reintegration.

sources.citation: OAS 2000 special session; US State Department 2000

2001.assessment:
Full integration restored. Toledo government celebrated internationally as
democratic success story. US, EU, IFI engagement increased. OAS Inter-
American Democratic Charter signed September 2001 (Lima Declaration hosted
in Peru). Active international participation. CVR internationally supported.

sources.citation: OAS Inter-American Democratic Charter September 2001; US State Department 2001

2002.assessment:
Full international integration. Active multilateral participation. US-Peru
FTA negotiations launched. Growing engagement with China. Normal relations
across the board.

2003.assessment:
Full integration. CVR process internationally recognized and supported.
Normal diplomatic relations. Active in regional organizations. No disputes.

2004.assessment:
Full integration. Peru contributing peacekeepers to UN missions (Haiti-MINUSTAH).
Normal bilateral and multilateral engagement. No disputes.

sources.citation: UN MINUSTAH troop contributions; US State Department 2004

2005.assessment:
Full integration. US-Peru FTA signed. APEC member (Pacific Alliance forerunner).
Active in multiple international organizations. Normal diplomatic relations.

2006.assessment:
Full integration. Garcia second term maintained strong international profile.
US, EU, China, Japan all active partners. Investment grade trajectory enhancing
financial integration. Active in OAS, UN, WTO, Andean Community, UNASUR.

2007.assessment:
Full integration. Peru hosted APEC Leaders Summit November 2008 (preparation
in 2007). US FTA ratified by Congress 2007. China FTA negotiations ongoing.
Active multilateral engagement. No disputes.

2008.assessment:
Full integration. Peru hosted APEC Leaders Summit November 2008. Highest
diplomatic profile year in recent history. Lima as meeting place. Active
international engagement across all dimensions.

sources.citation: APEC Lima Summit November 2008; US State Department 2008

2009.assessment:
Full integration with minor concerns. US FTA entered force February 2009.
China FTA signed April 2009 (entered force March 2010). Baguazo generated
international indigenous rights concern; ILO monitoring. No diplomatic
isolation.

2010.assessment:
Full integration. China FTA in force March 2010 — Peru major beneficiary
of China-LAC relationship. Pacific Alliance concept beginning. Active
multilateral participation. Investment grade firmly established.

2011.assessment:
Full integration. Pacific Alliance founded June 2012 (Peru founding member
with Chile, Colombia, Mexico — preparation 2011). Humala government maintained
international alignment. Active in OAS, UN, WTO, UNASUR, CAN. No significant
diplomatic disputes.

sources.citation: Pacific Alliance Declaration 2011; US State Department 2011

2012.assessment:
Full integration. Pacific Alliance formally established June 2012 — Peru
founding member. Japan, South Korea, Canada as observer states. Active
international engagement. Multiple FTAs network extensive.

sources.citation: Pacific Alliance June 2012; US State Department 2012

2013.assessment:
Full integration. TPP negotiations actively participated. Normal diplomatic
relations. Peru increasingly prominent in regional affairs through Pacific
Alliance.

2014.assessment:
Full integration. Bolivia-Peru relations somewhat tense (Bolivia's bid for
sea access through Chile affecting regional dynamics). Normal relations.
Active multilateral participation.

2015.assessment:
Full integration. Lima hosted COP20 climate conference (December 2014).
TPP negotiations nearly complete. Normal international engagement across
all dimensions.

2016.assessment:
Full integration. TPP signed February 2016 (later abandoned by US). PPK
government strongly internationally engaged. Pacific Alliance active. Normal
bilateral relations with all major partners.

2017.assessment:
Full integration. US withdrew from TPP January 2017; CPTPP negotiations
continued with Peru as member. Normal diplomatic relations. Odebrecht scandal
had international dimensions but no diplomatic isolation of Peru.

2018.assessment:
Full integration. CPTPP signed March 2018 (entered force December 2018).
Vizcarra internationally praised for anti-corruption. Lima Group formed
(September 2017) on Venezuela crisis — Peru founding member. Active regional
leadership.

sources.citation: CPTPP March 2018; Lima Group; US State Department 2018

2019.assessment:
Full integration. Lima Group active on Venezuela (Guaido recognition). Peru
recognized Guaido January 2019. OAS, US recognized constitutional process.
Active international engagement. No isolation.

2020.assessment:
Full integration maintained through COVID and political crisis. Three presidents
crisis internationally monitored but Peru's constitutional resolution accepted.
OAS, US, EU maintained normal relations with Sagasti government. COVID-era
multilateral coordination active.

2021.assessment:
Full integration. Castillo election internationally validated. Some uncertainty
about policy direction. Normal UN, OAS, regional participation. Multiple FTAs
network active. No isolation.

sources.citation: OAS Electoral Observation 2021; US State Department 2021

2022.assessment:
Full integration with some concern. Castillo self-coup (December 7) condemned
internationally but Boluarte's constitutional succession accepted. IACHR
concerned about protest deaths. Peru's OAS standing maintained. Normal trade
and FTA relationships.

sources.citation: OAS Peru 2022; IACHR 2022; US State Department 2022

2023.assessment:
Full integration with human rights concerns. IACHR formally expressed concern
about protest deaths and impunity. US Congress held hearings on Peru democracy.
Some European parliaments passed resolutions. Normal trade and diplomatic
relations maintained. No formal isolation.

2024.assessment:
Full integration. Normal diplomatic relations. Human rights concerns continuing
but no diplomatic isolation. FTA network active. Pre-2026 election positioning.

2025.assessment:
Full integration expected. Pre-election year; normal international engagement.
FTA network maintained. No expected isolation.

1995.assessment:
FDI ~4.6% of GDP. Privatization wave under Fujimori attracting massive inflows.
Telecom (Telefonica), mining, banking sectors privatized with foreign investment.
Political stability from SL defeat encouraging investors. Low by later mining
boom standards but driven by privatization rather than organic investment.
US, UK, Spanish investors dominant.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1996.assessment:
FDI ~5.0% of GDP. Peak privatization period. Largest deals concluded.
Antamina copper mine concession awarded. Yanacocha gold mine expansion.
Banking sector foreign investment. Electric utilities privatized.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1997.assessment:
FDI ~4.2% of GDP. Post-privatization normalization. Still high from
ongoing mining projects (Antamina construction began). Asian crisis effects
beginning H2 1997 but full impact not yet on flows.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1998.assessment:
FDI ~3.2% of GDP. Decline from Asian/El Nino crisis. Privatization
essentially complete; pipeline thinner. Political uncertainty (Fujimori
third-term) adding to investor hesitancy. Mining projects in construction
phase sustaining some inflows.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1999.assessment:
FDI ~2.2% of GDP. Continued decline. Political uncertainty (fraudulent 2000
elections preparation) deterring investment. Asian/Brazil crisis effects.
Low commodity prices reducing mining attractiveness.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2000.assessment:
FDI ~1.7% of GDP. Political crisis (Fujimori collapse, Vladivideo) caused
further FDI decline. Investment decisions delayed pending political resolution.
Transitional government (Paniagua) reassured investors but short tenure.
Near trough.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2001.assessment:
FDI ~1.9% of GDP. Modest recovery with Toledo democratic restoration.
International investors reassured by democratic transition. 9/11 global
uncertainty limited recovery. Mining exploration pipeline rebuilding.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2002.assessment:
FDI ~2.2% of GDP. Recovering. China commodity demand beginning to drive
mining investment. Stability under Toledo attracting green-field mining
investment. Construction phase projects in planning.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2003.assessment:
FDI ~2.0% of GDP. Moderate flows. Mining investment growing. Toledo
political difficulties somewhat dampening but economic fundamentals
improving. Investment grade trajectory building confidence.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2004.assessment:
FDI ~2.7% of GDP. Accelerating. Commodity boom driving mining investment.
Multiple new mine approvals. Copper, gold, zinc projects in development.
Investment grade credit improving.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2005.assessment:
FDI ~3.3% of GDP. Strong growth. Mining boom driving investment. Investment
grade achieved. Competitive tax terms for extractive sector. Multiple
large-scale mining projects in construction.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2006.assessment:
FDI ~3.8% of GDP. Strong. Garcia's election did not deter investors as he
moved to center. Commodity prices high. Mining, telecom, finance all attracting
inflows. FTA with US signed improving investment climate.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2007.assessment:
FDI ~4.7% of GDP. Very high. Commodity super-cycle at peak; Peru among
most attractive mining investment destinations globally. Multiple multi-billion-dollar
projects in development (Conga, Tia Maria, Las Bambas in planning). Cerro
Verde, Antamina expansions.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2008.assessment:
FDI ~5.0% of GDP. Peak mining era investment. Multiple major projects
in various stages. Global financial crisis H2 2008 beginning to affect
new investment decisions but existing projects continued.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2009.assessment:
FDI ~4.3% of GDP. Moderate decline from crisis but still very strong.
Existing mining projects continued construction. New approvals slower.
Peru resilient relative to regional peers. China FTA signed reinforcing
investment flows.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2010.assessment:
FDI ~5.2% of GDP. Recovery and new high. Commodity prices recovering.
Large-scale mining projects (Las Bambas, Constancia, Toromocho) approved.
China investors increasingly prominent. Record investment environment.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2011.assessment:
FDI ~4.7% of GDP. Strong. Humala election created brief investor concern
but mining projects continued. Commodity prices still elevated. Multiple
large projects (Las Bambas, Toromocho) in active construction.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2012.assessment:
FDI ~5.9% of GDP. All-time high. Large mining projects in peak construction
spend phase. Las Bambas, Toromocho, Constancia all active. Cerro Verde
expansion. Peru: $12B+ in FDI inflows. Global commodity investment near
peak.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2013.assessment:
FDI ~5.0% of GDP. Declining from 2012 peak as mega-project construction
phases wound down. Commodity prices falling from 2011 peaks. Still very
high by historical and regional standards.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2014.assessment:
FDI ~4.1% of GDP. Continued decline. Post-boom investment adjustment.
New mining project approvals slowing (Tia Maria, Conga delayed/blocked
by social conflict). Commodity prices declining. Services and retail
FDI partially offsetting mining decline.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2015.assessment:
FDI ~3.7% of GDP. Continued moderation. Las Bambas construction completion
reduced construction-phase inflows. New investment pipeline limited. Commodity
prices near cycle low. Social conflict around Tia Maria blocking new approvals.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2016.assessment:
FDI ~3.5% of GDP. Stable. Las Bambas operational since 2016; ongoing operational
investment. PPK government improving investment climate. Commodity prices
recovering. Retail, services, infrastructure FDI compensating for mining decline.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2017.assessment:
FDI ~3.1% of GDP. Declining. Political crisis (PPK-Fujimorismo) dampening
investment. El Nino Costero disruption. Odebrecht scandal affecting public
infrastructure investment approval. Organic private investment in services
maintaining some flows.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2018.assessment:
FDI ~3.0% of GDP. Stable. Vizcarra anti-corruption government reassured
investors. Infrastructure investment resumed (PPP projects). Services, retail,
finance continuing FDI. Mining exploration increasing.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2019.assessment:
FDI ~2.7% of GDP. Slight decline. Constitutional crisis (Congress dissolution)
created uncertainty. Global trade war headwinds. Pre-COVID last normal year.
Mining exploration active; new approvals slow.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2020.assessment:
FDI ~1.4% of GDP. Sharp COVID decline. Global FDI collapsed. Peruvian
lockdown disrupted operations. Three-presidents crisis. Investment decisions
suspended. Mining continued (essential) but new approvals frozen.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2021; World Bank WDI

2021.assessment:
FDI ~2.6% of GDP. Recovery. Economy rebounding. Copper price record boosting
mining sector. Castillo election created uncertainty; some investors cautious.
Existing projects continuing; new approvals uncertain.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2022; World Bank WDI

2022.assessment:
FDI ~2.9% of GDP. Moderate recovery. Despite Castillo crisis (December 2022),
mining sector investment ongoing. Boluarte government pragmatic on investment.
Copper prices elevated. Quellaveco (Anglo American) operational 2022 driving
operational investment.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2023; World Bank WDI

2023.assessment:
FDI ~2.3% of GDP. Moderate. Post-Castillo instability deterring new investment
decisions. Mining blockades H1 2023 highlighting risks. Boluarte government
trying to reassure investors. GDP recession limiting reinvestment flows.

2024.assessment:
FDI ~2.5% of GDP estimated. Recovery with economic growth (~3%). New mining
project approvals progressing (Tia Maria finally approved 2024). Boluarte
government actively promoting investment. Mining pipeline rebuilding.

2025.assessment:
FDI ~2.7% of GDP projected. Moderate. Mining pipeline recovery. Pre-election
year (2026); some uncertainty about post-election policy direction. Overall
moderate investment environment.

1995.assessment:
No foreign military presence; full sovereignty. Cenepa War with Ecuador
(January-February 1995) was a bilateral conflict on Peru's own territory;
no foreign intervention. US maintained counternarcotics advisors and
DEA presence under bilateral agreements — limited invited advisory role.
No foreign troops on Peruvian soil in combat roles.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1995; US DOD counternarcotics programs

1996.assessment:
No foreign military presence beyond invited US counternarcotics advisors.
Full sovereignty. Cenepa War peace process underway. MRTA hostage crisis
(December 1996) at Japanese Embassy; Japanese government consulted but
Peru retained full operational control of response.

1997.assessment:
Full sovereignty. MRTA hostage crisis resolved April 1997 entirely by
Peruvian forces. US counternarcotics cooperation ongoing. No significant
foreign military presence.

1998.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Ecuador-Peru peace treaty negotiations active. US counternarcotics
advisors present but limited. No foreign military forces.

1999.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Ecuador-Peru peace finalized (Brasilia Accord 1998 implemented).
US counternarcotics advisors ongoing. No foreign military forces.

2000.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Political crisis handled entirely by domestic actors.
No foreign military intervention. US counternarcotics cooperation continued.
Transitional government fully independent.

2001.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US counternarcotics cooperation strengthened under Toledo.
Peru contributed peacekeepers to MINUSTAH (Haiti) preparation. No foreign
forces on Peruvian territory in combat roles.

2002.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US counternarcotics cooperation. No foreign military presence.
Peru contributing to UN peacekeeping (MINUSTAH). Normal bilateral military
cooperation agreements.

2003.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military forces. US military cooperation
ongoing under bilateral agreements. Peru contributing peacekeepers internationally.

2004.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Peru one of largest contributors to MINUSTAH Haiti
peacekeeping. US counternarcotics advisors. No foreign military presence
on Peruvian soil.

sources.citation: UN MINUSTAH records; IISS Military Balance 2004

2005.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign forces on territory. US cooperation agreements.
Peru contributing to MINUSTAH and other UN missions.

2006.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Garcia second term maintained US military cooperation.
No foreign forces. Normal bilateral defense relationships with US, Brazil.
Peru active UN peacekeeping contributor.

2007.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US military cooperation, Joint exercises. No foreign
military presence. Normal defense relationships.

2008.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US military cooperation agreements. Joint counternarcotics
operations. No combat foreign presence. APEC hosted — demonstrating full
sovereignty and international standing.

2009.assessment:
Full sovereignty. US counternarcotics cooperation continued. VRAEM (Valley
of the Apurimac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers) anti-drug operations — Peruvian
forces only; US providing intelligence/equipment support under agreements.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009; US DOD counternarcotics

2010.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Peru modernizing military with purchases from Russia,
Korea, others. US cooperation maintained. No foreign military presence.

2011.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Humala, as former military officer, maintained professional
military with civilian control. US cooperation agreements renegotiated.
No foreign forces on territory.

2012.assessment:
Full sovereignty. VRAEM counterinsurgency operations ongoing against SL
remnants; entirely Peruvian military operation. US providing some intelligence
support. No foreign forces.

2013.assessment:
Full sovereignty. SL VRAEM leadership captured (2012-2013 operations
by Peruvian forces). US counternarcotics advisors. No foreign military
presence.

2014.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Normal bilateral military cooperation. No foreign forces.
US counternarcotics advisors under agreement.

2015.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Normal defense relationships. US counternarcotics cooperation.
No foreign forces.

2016.assessment:
Full sovereignty. PPK government maintained strong US military cooperation.
No foreign forces on territory. Normal bilateral defense agreements.

2017.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign forces. US counternarcotics cooperation.
Normal bilateral military relations.

2018.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Vizcarra maintained professional military relationships.
No foreign forces on territory. US cooperation agreements active.

2019.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign forces. Constitutional crisis handled by domestic
civilian and military institutions. Military firmly under civilian control
— did not intervene in Vizcarra-Congress crisis.

2020.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Military deployed for COVID lockdown enforcement under
civilian authority. Three-presidents crisis did not involve military
intervention — military recognized constitutional succession. Police
used in protests (November 2020).

2021.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Military remained professional under Castillo. Despite
Castillo's radical rhetoric, military upheld institutional norms. US
counternarcotics cooperation continued. No foreign forces.

2022.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Military refused Castillo's self-coup orders (December 7, 2022)
— a critical demonstration of institutional integrity. Military recognized
Boluarte as constitutional president. Security forces used in post-coup
protests (60+ killed). No foreign military involvement.

2023.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Military under Boluarte government; security forces
involved in protest management. No foreign military presence. US bilateral
cooperation maintained.

2024.assessment:
Full sovereignty. Normal military posture. No foreign forces. US cooperation
agreements maintained. Professional military under civilian control.

2025.assessment:
Full sovereignty expected. Pre-election year; military remains professional.
No anticipated foreign military involvement. Normal bilateral defense
cooperation.

1995.assessment:
Minimal displacement. SL (Sendero Luminoso) conflict declining rapidly after
Guzman capture (1992) but some residual displacement in Ayacucho, VRAEM
highlands. Internal displacement from conflict era (1980-1992 peak) still
present but new outflows minimal. No significant international refugee
outflows. Cenepa War (January-February 1995) caused minimal border displacement.

1996.assessment:
Minimal displacement. SL insurgency largely contained. MRTA hostage crisis
(December 1996) at Japanese Embassy — limited civilian impact. Historical
conflict IDPs returning to highland communities with government resettlement
programs. Net displacement minimal.

1997.assessment:
Minimal displacement. MRTA hostage crisis resolved April 1997 with no
significant civilian displacement. SL VRAEM remnants minimal. Historical
IDP return process ongoing. Overall population stability.

1998.assessment:
Minimal displacement. No significant conflict-related displacement. El Nino
caused some temporary internal displacement (flooding, landslides) but not
counted as conflict/political displacement. Stable population.

1999.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Political crisis (Fujimori third-term) not causing
displacement. Some political migrants from neighboring countries (Colombia,
Venezuela later) but minimal. No significant outflows.

2000.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Fujimori collapse (November 2000) and democratic
transition did not cause displacement. No political violence displacement.
Some Colombian refugees arriving in Peru (Colombian conflict). Net population
flows stable.

2001.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Democratic restoration peaceful. CVR process working
with IDP communities. No new displacement sources. Peru hosting Colombian
refugees fleeing paramilitary and FARC violence.

2002.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. Growing economic migration (domestic rural-to-urban)
but no forced displacement. Colombian refugee population in Peru modest.

2003.assessment:
Minimal displacement. CVR report delivered August 2003 documenting historic
displacement (1980-2000) but no new displacement sources. Overall
population stability.

2004.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. No conflict or political violence creating
displacement. Economic migration internal and international continues but
not forced displacement.

2005.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable population dynamics. Growing prosperity
reducing push factors for emigration. No conflict displacement.

2006.assessment:
Minimal displacement. No conflict. Garcia second term no displacement.
Economic boom reducing poverty and emigration incentives.

2007.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Strong economic growth. No forced displacement.
Some Peruvians returning from abroad with improving domestic economy.

2008.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Peak economic year. Some net returns. No conflict
displacement. Global financial crisis H2 2008 did not create displacement.

2009.assessment:
Minimal displacement but slight increase. Baguazo (June 2009) caused some
temporary displacement in Amazon Bagua region; indigenous communities
temporarily displaced during and after clashes (~34 deaths). No sustained
displacement. VRAEM SL minor activity causing some rural insecurity.

2010.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Post-Baguazo normalization. Economic recovery strong.
No conflict displacement. Stable population.

2011.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Humala government. Conga mining conflict caused
some local tension in Cajamarca but no significant displacement.
Stable national population dynamics.

2012.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. Some SL VRAEM activity creating local insecurity
but not significant displacement. Mining conflicts managed. Overall stable.

2013.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. SL VRAEM leadership captures reducing threat.
No significant conflict-related displacement.

2014.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. No conflict. Economic growth slowing but
not causing displacement pressures.

2015.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. No conflict-related displacement. El Nino
2015-2016 causing some natural disaster displacement but not political.

2016.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. PPK government. No conflict. Venezuela crisis
beginning to generate first Venezuelans transiting through Peru.

2017.assessment:
Minimal domestic displacement. El Nino Costero (January-March 2017) caused
significant temporary natural disaster displacement (~160,000 affected).
Venezuelan migration to Peru accelerating but as receiving country. No
political conflict displacement outflows from Peru.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistics 2017; OCHA El Nino Costero

2018.assessment:
Minimal domestic displacement. Peru now a major RECEIVING country for
Venezuelan migrants and refugees — over 500,000 Venezuelans in Peru by
end 2018. No significant Peruvian outflows. Political stability maintained
through PPK-Vizcarra transition.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistics 2018; R4V Venezuela refugee platform

2019.assessment:
Minimal Peruvian displacement outflows. Peru hosting 860,000+ Venezuelans
(second largest host after Colombia). Refugee policy generally open but
visa requirements introduced. Constitutional crisis (Congress dissolution)
did not cause displacement.

2020.assessment:
Minimal Peruvian displacement. COVID caused some internal movement (workers
walking home from Lima during lockdown — dramatic crisis of informal workers).
Some Venezuelans returned home due to lockdown hardships. Political crisis
(three presidents) did not cause political displacement.

2021.assessment:
Minimal Peruvian displacement. Castillo election uncertainty did not cause
significant emigration (unlike business elite emigration fears). Peru
continuing to host ~1.2M Venezuelans. No conflict displacement.

2022.assessment:
Slight increase in displacement. Castillo self-coup (December 7) and subsequent
protests caused internal displacement in affected southern/highland regions
(Puno, Apurimac, Ayacucho). ~60+ deaths; communities disrupted. Some families
temporarily displaced. Not major national displacement. Still minimal by
global standards.

2023.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Post-protest normalization. Some lingering insecurity
in Puno region. Mining blockades causing economic hardship but not significant
displacement. Peru continuing to host ~1.5M Venezuelans.

2024.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Stable. No conflict. Peru maintaining large Venezuelan
refugee hosting (~1.5M+). Pre-election year; no displacement drivers.

2025.assessment:
Minimal displacement expected. Pre-2026 election year; no significant
conflict or displacement drivers anticipated. Stable population dynamics.

1995.assessment:
No sanctions against Peru. Fujimori government recognized internationally;
US maintained normal relations despite human rights concerns. IMF program
compliance ensured no financial restrictions. Normal trade and investment
relations. Some international criticism of political practices but no
formal economic restrictions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights 1995; OAS records

1996.assessment:
No sanctions. Normal international relations maintained. US-Peru bilateral
relations normal; counternarcotics cooperation central. MRTA hostage crisis
(December 1996) generated international attention but no sanctions. IMF
program on track.

1997.assessment:
No formal sanctions. Some US Congressional pressure over Ivcher case and
Constitutional Tribunal dismissals. Verbal condemnations from OAS and US
State Department. No formal economic sanctions imposed. Trade and investment
relations normal.

1998.assessment:
No sanctions. International community monitoring but not acting. US prioritizing
counternarcotics cooperation. IFI loans continuing. Normal trade.

1999.assessment:
No sanctions. Growing international criticism of Fujimori third-term bid
and election manipulation. OAS electoral observation mission critical of
conditions. US, EU expressed concerns. No formal sanctions; trade and
financial relations normal.

sources.citation: OAS Electoral Observation Mission 1999; US State Department

2000.assessment:
No formal sanctions despite electoral fraud. OAS sent mission that rejected
election results; US expressed 'deep concern'. No financial sanctions imposed.
Vladivideo scandal collapse prompted rapid international response but through
diplomatic pressure not sanctions. Transitional government restored relations.
International community rewarded democratic transition with increased engagement.

sources.citation: OAS Permanent Council resolutions 2000; US State Department 2000

2001.assessment:
No sanctions. Toledo's democratic government received strong international
support. US aid increased; IFI engagement resumed. Peru invited back to
full international participation. No restrictions of any kind.

2002.assessment:
No sanctions. Normal international engagement. US-Peru FTA negotiations beginning.
IFI support strong. No restrictions.

2003.assessment:
No sanctions. International relations fully normalized. CVR process
internationally supported. No restrictions.

2004.assessment:
No sanctions. Normal international relations. US-Peru FTA negotiations
ongoing. No restrictions of any kind.

2005.assessment:
No sanctions. US-Peru FTA signed April 2006 (ratified later). Normal bilateral
and multilateral relations. No restrictions.

2006.assessment:
No sanctions. Garcia government maintained strong US and international
relationships. US FTA ratified by US Congress 2007, entered force 2009.
Normal international relations. No restrictions.

2007.assessment:
No sanctions. China FTA negotiations beginning (signed 2009). Normal
international relations. No restrictions.

2008.assessment:
No sanctions. Normal international relations across all major partners.
No restrictions of any kind.

2009.assessment:
No sanctions despite Baguazo controversy. US, EU expressed concern about
indigenous rights. No formal sanctions. US-Peru FTA entered into force
February 2009. No restrictions.

2010.assessment:
No sanctions. China FTA entered force March 2010. Normal international
relations. No restrictions.

2011.assessment:
No sanctions. Humala election caused brief market concern but no international
sanctions response. Normal bilateral and multilateral engagement. No restrictions.

2012.assessment:
No sanctions. Normal international relations. Conga mining controversy
drew some NGO international attention but no sanctions. No restrictions.

2013.assessment:
No sanctions. CPTPP/TPP negotiations included Peru. Normal international
engagement. No restrictions.

2014.assessment: No sanctions. Normal international relations. No restrictions.

2015.assessment:
No sanctions. TPP signed (February 2016). Normal international relations.
No restrictions.

2016.assessment:
No sanctions. PPK government maintained strong international relationships.
Normal bilateral and multilateral engagement. No restrictions.

2017.assessment:
No sanctions against Peru. Odebrecht scandal implicated international
executives but no sanctions on Peru itself. CPTPP signed (January 2018).
Normal international relations.

2018.assessment:
No sanctions. Vizcarra anti-corruption government well-regarded internationally.
CPTPP entered force December 2018. Normal international relations. No restrictions.

2019.assessment:
No sanctions. Constitutional crisis (Congress dissolution) internationally
monitored; US and OAS observed process. No sanctions imposed. Normal
international relations maintained through political turbulence.

2020.assessment:
No sanctions. Political crisis (three presidents) observed internationally.
OAS, US, EU monitored but recognized Sagasti's constitutional assumption of
presidency. COVID-related restrictions globally but no specific sanctions
on Peru. Normal trade and investment flows maintained.

2021.assessment:
No sanctions. Castillo's election outcome internationally validated by OAS
and US despite Fujimorista fraud allegations. Normal international relations.
Some business community concern about Castillo policy direction but no
official sanctions.

sources.citation: OAS Electoral Observation Peru 2021; US State Department 2021

2022.assessment:
No sanctions. Castillo self-coup (December 7) condemned internationally but
Peru's constitutional response (arrest, impeachment, Boluarte succession)
accepted as legal. IACHR expressed concern about protest crackdown but no
sanctions imposed. Normal trade and investment.

sources.citation: OAS resolution on Peru December 2022; US State Department 2022

2023.assessment:
No formal sanctions. IACHR and international human rights bodies expressed
concern about 60+ protest deaths and impunity. US State Department human
rights report critical. Congressional immunity legislation drew international
criticism. No formal economic sanctions.

2024.assessment:
No sanctions. US, EU continued normal relations despite human rights and
anti-corruption concerns. Boluarte government maintained functional international
relationships. No restrictions. Pre-2026 election period.

2025.assessment:
No sanctions expected. Peru maintaining normal international relations.
FTA network stable. No evidence of impending restrictions.

1995.assessment:
Fujimori's Peru rated CL=4 (Partly Free). Significant restrictions on civil
liberties under Fujimori's semi-authoritarian rule. Press operated under
self-censorship and government pressure; intelligence services monitored
opposition. Right to assembly limited; torture documented in counterinsurgency
operations. SIN under Montesinos conducted surveillance and intimidation of
journalists, politicians, and civil society.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995-1996

1996.assessment:
CL=4 maintained. Increased authoritarian consolidation. Montesinos-controlled
SIN expanded surveillance and media manipulation. Tabloid newspapers ('chicha
press') on government payroll to discredit opposition. MRTA hostage crisis
at Japanese Embassy (December 1996 - April 1997). Judiciary purged; press
freedom continued to deteriorate as Fujimori regime bought media owners.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996-1997

1997.assessment:
CL=4. Baruch Ivcher (Frecuencia Latina owner) stripped of citizenship after
broadcasting SIN corruption stories (1997) — landmark case of media suppression.
Constitutional Tribunal justices dismissed for opposing third-term bid (1997).
Press freedom severely curtailed; government consolidated control over major
TV channels through ownership manipulation and SIN leverage.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997-1998; Human Rights Watch, Peru country reports

1998.assessment:
CL=4. Continuing systematic civil liberties restrictions. SIN surveillance of
political opponents, judges, and journalists documented. Independent media faced
advertising boycotts coordinated by government. Judiciary remained under executive
influence. Human rights defenders faced harassment. Municipal opposition leaders
monitored and intimidated.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998-1999

1999.assessment:
CL=5 (Not Free boundary). Fujimori third-term candidacy and election preparations
tightened authoritarian controls. Electoral observation restricted; Ombudsman
marginalized. Opposition protests escalated but faced repression. Vladivideo
scandal about to break — widespread bribery of politicians, judges, business
leaders, and media owners documented on video. Civil liberties at nadir of
Fujimori period.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000

2000.assessment:
CL=5. Year of Fujimori's collapse. Fraudulent April 2000 elections; massive
protests (Marcha de los Cuatro Suyos, July 2000). Vladivideo scandal broke
September 2000, revealing systematic corruption and repression. Fujimori fled
to Japan in November 2000, faxed resignation. Transitional government under
Paniagua began restoring civil liberties. Year split: severe restrictions
early 2000, improving from November 2000.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-2001; McClintock & Vallas (2003) The United States and Peru

2001.assessment:
CL=3 (Partly Free, significantly improved). Democratic restoration under Toledo
(elected June 2001). Press freedom dramatically recovered — independent media
resumed operation, chicha press collapsed without government subsidies. SIN
dissolved; human rights prosecutions began. Truth and Reconciliation Commission
(CVR) established June 2001. Freedom of assembly restored; civil society
organizations active. Significant improvement but legacy institutional weaknesses
remained.
