sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-2002

2002.assessment:
CL=3. Consolidation of democratic liberties. CVR hearings ongoing; significant
public testimonies about Fujimori-era abuses. Independent press active and
investigative. Religious freedom full; assembly rights restored. Some concerns:
Toledo government faced strikes and protests which it managed with varying
effectiveness. Police use of force in protest dispersal occasionally excessive.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-2003

2003.assessment:
CL=3. CVR delivered final report August 2003, documenting ~70,000 deaths
1980-2000. Toledo government's approval ratings collapsed amid corruption
allegations but civil liberties not restricted. Press free and investigative.
Labor rights generally respected. Some protests against mining projects; police
response occasionally disproportionate. Indigenous communities' rights in
practice limited despite legal protections.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-2004; CVR Final Report 2003

2004.assessment:
CL=3. Stable civil liberties situation. Toledo presidency continued with low
approval but without authoritarian restrictions. Press freedom maintained;
civil society active. Social conflicts over mining increasing (Tintaya,
Tambogrande). Anti-mining protests occasionally resulted in clashes. Overall
democratic freedoms functioning despite political turbulence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-2005

2005.assessment:
CL=3. Final year of Toledo administration. Civil liberties stable. Presidential
campaign for 2006 elections active with competitive press coverage of multiple
candidates (Garcia, Humala, Flores-Nano). Freedom of assembly and expression
functioning. Some regional protests against mining. Ethnic/indigenous rights
advancing in rhetoric but implementation patchy.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-2006

2006.assessment:
CL=3. Garcia elected in runoff over Humala (June 2006). Competitive elections
with free press coverage. Civil liberties maintained. Garcia's second government
began with pro-market, pro-US orientation different from first term. Some
concerns about Garcia's use of anti-terrorism decrees against protest. Overall
democratic freedoms functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-2007

2007.assessment:
CL=3. Garcia government used legislative decrees to fast-track FTA with US,
including controversial decrees affecting indigenous land rights. Strong economic
growth; civil liberties maintained overall. Amazon indigenous communities
organized against land-use decrees. Press free and critical of government.
Human rights organizations active. Some labor disputes.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-2008

2008.assessment:
CL=3. Amazon indigenous communities protesting Garcia's legislative decrees
granting land concessions ahead of US FTA implementation. Awajun and Wampis
indigenous blockades; protests at Bagua escalating. Press freedom maintained.
Overall civil liberties score stable but growing tensions around indigenous
rights and extractive industry expansion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-2009

2009.assessment:
CL=3. Baguazo (June 5, 2009): clashes between indigenous protesters and police
at Curva del Diablo near Bagua resulted in ~34 deaths (24 police, 10+ indigenous).
Garcia government's use of force and media narrative controversial. Decrees
later repealed. Despite violence, Freedom House maintained CL=3 as isolated
incident in otherwise functioning democratic context. Press coverage of event
wide; some journalists faced harassment.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-2010; Defensoría del Pueblo (2009) Informe sobre el Baguazo

2010.assessment:
CL=3. Post-Baguazo period; some accountability investigations but no major
prosecutions of officials. Garcia government continued extractive industry
expansion. Strong economic performance. Press freedom maintained; civil society
active. Regional conflicts around Minas Conga and other mining projects
continuing. Pre-election year with multiple candidates campaigning freely.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-2011

2011.assessment:
CL=3. Humala elected June 2011 (former nationalist, moderated to centrist).
Competitive elections with free press. Humala's win surprised markets but
government transitioned peacefully. Civil liberties maintained. Mining conflicts
(Conga project in Cajamarca) intensified — Humala declared state of emergency
in Cajamarca November 2011. Some civil liberties concerns around protest suppression
in mining regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
CL=3. Conga mining conflict continued; protests in Cajamarca by anti-mining
activists led by regional president Gregorio Santos. States of emergency
invoked periodically in extractive conflict zones. Press freedom overall
maintained. Civil society organizations active in monitoring. Humala government
increasingly relied on emergency powers in conflict regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-2013

2013.assessment:
CL=3. Stable civil liberties. Gregorio Santos (Cajamarca) arrested on corruption
charges (critics alleged political motivation). Press freedom maintained; multiple
critical outlets operating. SL VRAEM activities minimal. Social conflicts around
extractive industry continuing but managed through negotiation and emergency
declarations. Overall democratic freedoms functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-2014

2014.assessment:
CL=3. Humala second half; declining popularity. Civil liberties stable. Odebrecht
corruption scandal beginning to emerge (Lava Jato). Press investigative capacity
active. Human rights organizations monitoring extractive conflicts. Labor rights
formally protected. Internet freedom growing; social media active in political
discourse.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014-2015

2015.assessment:
CL=3. Pre-election year with active campaigning. Keiko Fujimori leading polls;
PPK and Acuña also running. Press free; debates and coverage extensive. Lava
Jato/Odebrecht investigations expanding. Civil liberties maintained. Some
concerns about campaign financing opacity. Indigenous rights formally protected
with ILO Convention 169 implementation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-2016

2016.assessment:
CL=3. PPK won narrow runoff over Keiko Fujimori (June 2016). Fujimorismo
won majority in Congress creating immediate institutional tension. Press free;
civil liberties maintained. Odebrecht revelations accelerating; major
investigative journalism covering corruption. Civil society organizations
active. Democratic quality maintained despite political polarization.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016-2017

2017.assessment:
CL=3. Odebrecht/Lava Jato dominating political scene. PPK-Fujimorista Congress
conflict intensifying. PPK survived first impeachment motion December 2017
(pardoned Fujimori senior — controversial). Press freedom maintained; major
investigative coverage of Odebrecht. Civil society active in anti-corruption
protests. Freedom of assembly exercised extensively.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017-2018

2018.assessment:
CL=3. PPK resigned March 2018 (further Odebrecht revelations); Vizcarra assumed
presidency. Vizcarra launched anti-corruption agenda; Civil society mobilized
in support of judiciary independence (protests against 'Los Cuellos Blancos del
Puerto' corruption network). Press free; aggressive investigative journalism.
Fujimori humanitarian pardon annulled December 2018. Active civil society.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018-2019

2019.assessment:
CL=3. Vizcarra dissolved Congress September 2019 after it blocked anti-corruption
reforms using 'cuestión de confianza'. Constitutional crisis managed through
elections. Civil liberties maintained despite institutional turbulence. Press
free; significant protest activity. Anti-corruption momentum continued; several
high-profile arrests and extraditions (Toledo, García suicide arrest attempt).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019-2020

2020.assessment:
CL=3. Three presidents in one week (November 2020): Vizcarra impeached for
'moral incapacity'; Merino (Congress speaker) briefly assumed presidency;
massive protests erupted (Inti Sotelo and Bryan Pintado killed by police
November 14); Merino resigned; Sagasti assumed presidency. COVID lockdown
(world's strictest) impacted freedoms; 180,000+ COVID deaths. Civil liberties
score maintained CL=3 reflecting brief crisis followed by restoration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020-2021

2021.assessment:
CL=3. Castillo (rural teacher, leftist) narrowly defeated Keiko Fujimori in
disputed runoff (July 2021). Extremely tight result: 44,000 votes. Fujimorismo
contested results; OAS and international observers validated election. Castillo
government began amid institutional conflict. Press free and critical. Civil
liberties maintained. Some concerns about Castillo's associations with radical
advisors.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021-2022

2022.assessment:
CL=4. Castillo self-coup attempt December 7, 2022 — announced dissolution of
Congress and emergency government; arrested same day; Boluarte assumed presidency.
Major protests erupted December 2022 through January-February 2023; security
forces killed 60+ protesters (primarily in Puno and Andean regions). Inter-American
Commission on Human Rights condemned excessive force. Freedom House downgraded
Peru to CL=4 citing crackdown on protests and institutional erosion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-2023; IACHR (2023) Situation in Peru

2023.assessment:
CL=4. Boluarte government continued amid low approval ratings (~10%) and
ongoing anti-government sentiment. Investigations into protest deaths stalled;
impunity concerns for security forces. Congress passed laws limiting
prosecutorial independence. Press freedom under pressure; investigative
journalists facing harassment. Anti-corruption accountability weakened by
congressional actions. Civil liberties remained restricted compared to 2018-2021
baseline.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-2024

2024.assessment:
CL=4. Continuing civil liberties concerns. Congress passed legislation weakening
anti-corruption prosecutor (Fiscalía Especializada en Delitos de Corrupción);
interference in Odebrecht/Lava Jato prosecutions. Boluarte government corruption
scandals (Rolexgate). Press freedom monitored — some journalists facing legal
harassment. Civil society organizations active but facing increased scrutiny.
2026 elections approaching with fragmented political landscape.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024-2025

2025.assessment:
CL=4 estimated. Peru approaching 2026 elections. Boluarte government under
pressure; congressional coalition fragile. Anti-corruption institutions under
continued legislative pressure. Civil liberties expected to remain at CL=4
level given ongoing institutional weakening and absence of accountability for
2022-2023 protest deaths. Press freedom monitored by CPJ and RSF.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025 (preliminary)

1995.assessment:
Fujimori's ruling coalition externally unified but held together through
patronage, coercion, and Montesinos's SIN apparatus rather than genuine
consensus. Military loyal to Fujimori-Montesinos arrangement. Business elite
aligned due to economic stability and favorable policy. Political opposition
fragmented and marginalized. Apparent cohesion masked underlying tensions
— Montesinos operated shadow state separate from formal government.

sources.citation: Conaghan (2005) Fujimori's Peru: Deception in the Public Sphere

1996.assessment:
Coalition stability maintained through patronage networks. Military elite
kept loyal via promotions and institutional protection. Business community
aligned with economic boom. Opposition parties atomized — APRA weakened,
traditional right fragmented. MRTA hostage crisis (December 1996) tested
government but ultimately consolidated Fujimori's image as tough leader.
Montesinos held coalition together through videotaped blackmail material.

sources.citation: Conaghan (2005); Bowen & Holligan (2003) The Imperfect Spy

1997.assessment:
Emerging cracks in coalition. Constitutional Tribunal justices dismissed
for opposing third-term unconstitutionality ruling (1997) — signal of
increasing coercion within system. Baruch Ivcher (media owner) denaturalized
after broadcasting SIN abuses. Some business community concerns. Congress
majority still maintained but achieved through purchasing votes (as later
revealed in vladivideos). Coalition showing strain under pressure.

sources.citation: Conaghan (2005); Freedom House Country Report Peru 1997

1998.assessment:
Coalition maintenance required increasingly aggressive tactics. Vladivideos
later revealed extensive bribery of judges, politicians, business leaders,
and media — system operating on blackmail and payments. Military leadership
loyal but increasingly aware of Montesinos's independent power base. Opposition
preparing for 2000 elections; Toledo and others organizing. Underlying tensions
managed but fragility increasing.

sources.citation: Conaghan (2005); Degregori (2000) La Década de la Antipolítica

1999.assessment:
Elite cohesion fracturing as Fujimori's third-term bid faced international
criticism. Some business elite privately concerned about sustainability. OAS
observers and international community skeptical. Montesinos intensified
surveillance and blackmail operations. Some military officers quietly
uncomfortable. Opposition consolidating around Toledo; significant popular
protests. Coalition cohesion increasingly coercive rather than voluntary.

2000.assessment:
Complete elite fragmentation upon Vladivideo release (September 2000). System
collapsed rapidly: military abandoned Fujimori; Congress turned against him;
business community withdrew support; international community condemned regime.
Fujimori fled to Japan November 2000. Transitional government under Paniagua
assembled broad coalition — opposition, civil society, anti-Fujimori military
officers. Elite cohesion essentially absent as old order dissolved and new
democratic coalition formed.

sources.citation: Conaghan (2005); Crabtree (2001) Peru Under Toledo: The Political Economy of Democratic Revival

2001.assessment:
New democratic coalition forming around Toledo. Broad cross-party support
for democratic transition. Toledo government formed technocratic economic
team; civil society organizations energized. Military subordinated to civilian
control. Anti-Fujimori/Montesinos consensus provided temporary unity across
political spectrum. CVR established with multi-party support. Elite cohesion
around democratic restoration, though Toledo's Perú Posible party was narrow.

sources.citation: Crabtree & Durand (2017) Peru: Elite Power and Political Capture; Freedom House 2001

2002.assessment:
Toledo coalition showing strain as governing difficulties multiplied. Toledo
faced strikes and regional protests within first year. Congress opposition
growing; APRA and Fujimorista remnants obstructing. Economic team (Kuczynski
as prime minister) maintained pro-market direction over Toledo's nationalist
instincts. Business elite supportive of economic policy but skeptical of
Toledo personally. Fragmented congressional landscape impeding governance.

sources.citation: Crabtree (2001-2006 analysis); ICG Latin America Reports

2003.assessment:
Toledo's approval collapsed below 10% by mid-2003 amid strikes, corruption
allegations (his sister, associates), and economic management disputes.
Cabinet shuffles frequent. CVR delivery of final report August 2003 created
political tensions — military resistance to accountability. Congress
increasingly adversarial. Some calls for impeachment. However, democratic
institutions held; elite consensus remained on not returning to Fujimori-era
authoritarianism.

sources.citation: Cotler & Grompone (2000); Latin America Monitor Peru 2003

2004.assessment:
Toledo government in difficult mid-term period but no serious coup threat.
Military firmly under civilian control. Business elite focused on strong economic
performance (7%+ GDP growth beginning). APRA positioning for 2006 elections.
Fujimorista faction weakened by ongoing prosecutions. Regional political actors
growing in importance (decentralization 2002). Elite fragmentation at national
level but stable democratic consensus.

sources.citation: Levitsky & Mainwaring (2006) Organized Labor and Democracy in Latin America

2005.assessment:
Pre-election year with active coalition building. Garcia (APRA), Humala, and
Flores-Nano competing. Business elite uncertain about Humala's nationalism.
Toledo completing term with low approval but stable institutions. Democratic
competition vigorous. No coup risk; military professionalized. Regional elites
participating through newly empowered regional governments. Competitive,
fragmented but functional democratic elite landscape.

sources.citation: Tanaka (2011) Peru's Democratic Challenges; ICG Latin America 2005

2006.assessment:
Garcia won runoff (June 2006); business elite relief over Humala defeat.
Garcia assembled centrist-technocratic cabinet, signaling break from first
APRA term (1985-1990 economic disaster). Business community cautiously optimistic.
Fujimorismo (Keiko running for Congress) present but not disruptive. Military
professional. Regional governments active. Relatively cohesive governing
coalition with technocratic economic team (Luis Carranza as Finance Minister).

2007.assessment:
Garcia government with strong economic growth and relatively stable coalition.
Business elite aligned with pro-market policies. FTA negotiations with US
progressing (signed 2007, ratified 2009). Congress supportive though APRA
minority required coalition management. Military professional. Some tensions
with Amazon indigenous communities over land decrees but not elite-level
fragmentation. Functional governance with managed tensions.

sources.citation: Durand (2010) Business and Politics in Peru; ICG Latin America

2008.assessment:
Petrogate scandal (October 2008): García's government resigned en masse after
leaked audio recordings revealed oil contracts awarded under suspicious
circumstances. Elite cohesion temporarily disrupted; cabinet reconstituted.
Despite scandal, Garcia maintained enough coalition support. Business elite
loyal to economic continuity. Congress fragmented but governable. Amazon
indigenous opposition organizing against FTA-related land decrees.

2009.assessment:
Baguazo (June 2009) created significant elite divisions. Military/police used
against indigenous protesters; Garcia defended actions; Yehude Simon (PM)
resigned. Congress divided over response; Indigenist parties condemned government.
Church, Ombudsman, and civil society critical of government response. Business
elite split between those prioritizing extractive investment and those concerned
about reputational damage. Open factional tensions over Baguazo handling.

sources.citation: Defensoría del Pueblo (2009); ICG Latin America Baguazo analysis

2010.assessment:
Post-Baguazo normalization. Garcia government regained stability aided by
continued strong economic growth. Pre-election positioning for 2011. Humala
(nationalist, Chavez-linked in perception) leading some polls; business elite
concerned. Fujimorismo (Keiko) consolidating. APRA Garcia declined to run
again. Multiple elite factions positioning for 2011 — competitive but within
democratic frame.

2011.assessment:
Humala's election (June 2011) caused initial elite alarm — markets fell, business
community anxious. However Humala formed centrist cabinet (Salomón Lerner PM,
Castilla Finance Minister) and distanced from Chavez. Business community gradually
accepted. Keiko's Fujimorismo as strong opposition created adversarial Congress
dynamic. Conga mining conflict (Cajamarca) created tensions within government
between nationalist and technocratic factions. Lerner resigned November 2011.

sources.citation: Levitsky & Roberts (2011) The Resurgence of the Latin American Left; ICG

2012.assessment:
Humala government split between nationalist/social factions and technocratic
economic team. Ollanta Humala's wife Nadine Heredia perceived as shadow
power; created tensions. Conga project suspended amid protests and state of
emergency. Cabinet shuffles. Business community growing impatient with political
instability but economy still strong. Fujimorista opposition aggressive in
Congress. Open factional tensions within government over economic model.

sources.citation: ICG Latin America Report No. 44 (2012) Peru's Political Crisis

2013.assessment:
Humala government stabilizing around technocratic-centrist approach. Castilla
(Finance) maintained economic continuity; growth slowing somewhat. Fujimorista
opposition continued in Congress. Santos (Cajamarca) arrested on corruption.
Elite cohesion functional though Humala-Heredia relationship and original
nationalist base creating ongoing friction. No serious coup threat; democratic
institutions functioning.

sources.citation: Levitsky & Murillo (2013) Lessons from Latin America; Latin America Monitor

2014.assessment:
Humala government weakening as economy slowed and Odebrecht scandal began
surfacing. Approval ratings declining. Keiko Fujimori consolidating Fujimorismo
as dominant opposition force. PPK building centrist alliance. APRA weakened.
Elite landscape fragmented around multiple parties without strong institutional
roots. Odebrecht tentacles reaching across political spectrum — creating
underlying corruption exposure across elite factions.

sources.citation: Durand (2018) Odebrecht: La Empresa que Capturaba Gobiernos; Latin America Monitor

2015.assessment:
Pre-election year with five major presidential candidates. Fujimorismo (Keiko)
leading. Odebrecht having paid all major candidates — this would emerge in
2017-2018 investigations. Business elite divided between different factions;
mining elite preferred centrist candidates. Humala lame duck. Military professional
and non-interventionist. Competitive democratic fragmentation without authoritarian
threat.

sources.citation: Durand (2018); Vergara & Watanabe (2016) Peru: La Democracia en Cuestión

2016.assessment:
PPK won narrow runoff over Keiko (June 2016); Fujimorismo won congressional
majority (73 of 130 seats). Immediate adversarial dynamic: Keiko's faction
able to block and obstruct while PPK had no majority. Business elite aligned
with PPK's pro-market orientation. Open elite conflict between executive
(PPK) and legislative (Fujimorismo) from first days. Odebrecht investigations
beginning — PPK received Odebrecht funds, creating vulnerability.

2017.assessment:
Intense elite conflict. Fujimorismo drove first PPK impeachment motion (December
2017); PPK survived by pardoning Alberto Fujimori senior (controversial political
deal). Odebrecht confessions in Brazil naming Peruvian politicians accelerated.
PPK, Garcia, Humala, Toledo, Keiko all implicated in Odebrecht. Elite under
massive corruption investigation pressure. Active power struggles between
executive and Fujimorista Congress.

2018.assessment:
PPK resigned March 2018 (second Odebrecht revelations); Vizcarra assumed
presidency as anti-corruption reformer. New dynamic: Vizcarra vs Fujimorismo.
Fujimorismo split (Keiko under investigation; some Fujimoristas supported
anti-corruption agenda). Vizcarra referendum November 2018 on anti-corruption
reforms won overwhelmingly. Garcia arrested February 2019 (shot himself).
Ongoing active power struggle between reformist executive and Fujimorista/old-guard
Congress.

sources.citation: Vergara (2021) Polarización y Democracia; Freedom House Peru 2018-2019

2019.assessment:
Vizcarra dissolved Congress September 2019 after repeated 'cuestión de confianza'
manipulations by Fujimorista majority. Snap congressional elections January
2020. Extraordinary elite conflict: executive vs legislature each trying to
remove the other. Garcia killed himself in custody (April 2019). Keiko Fujimori
in and out of pre-trial detention. Odebrecht prosecutions ongoing. Peru's elite
essentially at war with itself through legal and constitutional means.

sources.citation: Crabtree & Durand (2017 updated); ICG Latin America

2020.assessment:
Complete elite fragmentation. Vizcarra impeached November 2020 for 'moral
incapacity' (corruption allegations). Merino (Congress speaker) became president;
cabinet refused to serve; massive protests killed two; Merino resigned after
six days. Emergency constitutional appointment of Sagasti (from Partido Morado).
Three presidents in one week demonstrated total elite incoherence. COVID
catastrophe (highest per-capita death rate globally at times) compounded crisis.
Elite completely fragmented with no stable coalition possible.

2021.assessment:
Castillo's narrow election victory (July 2021) after disputed runoff created
new elite fractures. Business elite, Lima establishment, Fujimorismo united
against Castillo (rural, indigenous, leftist — outside traditional elite).
Castillo cabinet extremely unstable (5 PMs in first year). Odebrecht prosecutions
continued. Keiko Fujimori initially refused to recognize results (briefly).
Congress adversarial from day one; three impeachment attempts in 16 months.
Elite completely fragmented with Castillo vs establishment dynamic.

sources.citation: Freedom House Peru 2021-2022; ICG Latin America

2022.assessment:
Elite conflict reached climax with Castillo self-coup attempt (December 7, 2022)
— announced dissolution of Congress; cabinet, attorney general, Constitutional
Tribunal, and military all refused to recognize attempt; Castillo arrested within
hours. Boluarte (VP) assumed presidency, initially from same leftist coalition
as Castillo but pivoted to right-wing Congress support. Protests killed 60+.
New elite alignment: Boluarte-conservative Congress coalition vs Castillo
loyalists and popular movement. Still highly fragmented.

2023.assessment:
Boluarte government with paradoxical coalition: former leftist allied with
right-wing and Fujimorista Congress to survive. Congressional elites protected
themselves by passing immunity laws, weakening anti-corruption prosecutors,
and blocking early elections. Odebrecht prosecutions and ex-president cases
continuing (Toledo extradited from US, Garcia dead, Fujimori Sr. released
then re-imprisoned). Elite fragmentation persisting but Boluarte-Congress
transactional alignment provided unstable governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House Peru 2023-2024; ICG Latin America

2024.assessment:
Boluarte 'Rolexgate' scandal (luxury watches as gifts) further eroded legitimacy.
Congressional elites accelerated immunity and anti-prosecution legislation.
2026 election positioning began with multiple candidates fragmented across
spectrum. Keiko Fujimori considering another run. Odebrecht cases against
Humala, others advancing. Peru's political elite remained highly fragmented
with no dominant coalition — governed through transactional arrangements
rather than principled alliances.

sources.citation: Freedom House Peru 2024-2025; Latin America Monitor Peru 2024

2025.assessment:
Elite fragmentation ongoing ahead of 2026 elections. Boluarte government
with low approval but congressional protection. Multiple presidential candidacies
emerging across fragmented political landscape. No dominant elite coalition.
Anti-corruption institutions under pressure from congressional legislation.
Peru's characteristic hyper-fragmentation of elite politics continuing with
no clear path to stable governance.

sources.citation: Latin America Monitor Peru 2025; ICG Latin America

1995.assessment:
Fujimori's second term (re-elected April 1995 with 64% in somewhat controlled
election). Congress functioned but within authoritarian constraints — Fujimori
dissolved Congress in 1992 self-coup (autogolpe), installed compliant Constituent
Assembly (CCD), then new Congress (CCD) allied with Fujimori. Judiciary under
executive control — illegal purges 1992. Constitutional Court had members dismissed.
Elections conducted but manipulated. SIN intelligence service (Montesinos) operated
as parallel state. Partial institutional functionality.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995-1996; Burt (2007) Political Violence and the Authoritarian State in Peru

1996.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially degraded. Congress controlled by Fujimori
coalition. Supreme Court under political influence. Constitutional Tribunal
dismissed magistrates attempting to rule on Fujimori re-election. Executive
dominance clear. Elections conducted with controls. SIN surveillance widespread.
Amnesty law passed protecting military human rights abusers. Partially functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996-1997

1997.assessment:
Constitutional Tribunal crisis — three magistrates dismissed for ruling
against Fujimori's third term eligibility (May 1997). Supreme Court under
political control. SIN (Montesinos) monitoring politicians, journalists.
Congress functioning but government-controlled. Increasing authoritarian
consolidation. Media ownership manipulation (Bloque Popular).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997-1998; Constitutional Tribunal dismissal

1998.assessment:
Institutional degradation continuing. Legislative process controlled. Judiciary
subordinate to executive. ONPE (electoral body) under government influence.
SIN infiltrated all institutions. Media manipulation intensifying. Civil society
organizations under surveillance and pressure. Technically functioning but
severely compromised.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998-1999

1999.assessment:
Pre-election year with Fujimori seeking third term. Constitutional Tribunal
non-functional (dismissed magistrates). OAS and opposition challenging electoral
conditions. ONPE under executive control. Media bribery systematic (vladi-videos
revealed later). Congress subordinate. Severely degraded institutional functioning.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000; ICG Peru reports

2000.assessment:
Severely degraded institutions through most of 2000. Fujimori re-elected in
deeply flawed election (May-June 2000). OAS pressured for democratization.
Vladivideo scandal (September 2000) exposed systematic institutional bribery —
Montesinos bribing politicians, judges, military, media. Fujimori fled to Japan
(November). Paniagua interim government began institutional restoration.
Year transitions from severely degraded to beginning of recovery.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-2001; OAS Election Monitoring

2001.assessment:
Rapid institutional restoration under interim President Paniagua and then
Toledo (elected April 2001, sworn July 2001). Constitutional Tribunal restored.
New electoral commission. Judiciary reform beginning. Montesinos arrested in
Venezuela (June 2001). Congress opposition (Fujimori support reduced). Democratic
institutions returning to functional status. Significant improvement from 2000.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-2002; CVR establishment

2002.assessment:
Institutions functioning but flawed. Congress competitive, judiciary reforming
(though corruption persistent). CVR operating. Regional elections establishing
new decentralized institutions (2003). Toledo government facing high social
unrest and declining approval but institutions holding. Electoral system
credible. Press free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-2003

2003.assessment:
Institutions functional but challenged. CVR Final Report (August 2003) — independent
commission functioning. Regional governments established. Judicial reform ongoing.
Toledo facing impeachment threats (never materialized). Congress independent.
Institutions functioning within democratic framework though weak compared to OECD.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-2004; CVR Final Report 2003

2004.assessment:
Functional but flawed institutions. Toledo government weak (approval ~8% at
times). Corruption allegations. Judicial system slow and corrupt but independent.
Elections next year (2006) — institutional preparation functioning. Congress
operating independently. Press free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-2005

2005.assessment:
Functional institutions managing pre-election period. Toledo survived despite
extremely low approval. Constitutional order maintained. Electoral commission
organizing 2006 elections. Judiciary functional though corrupt. JNE (electoral
tribunal) independent. Congress fully functional and independent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-2006

2006.assessment:
Institutions functioned well in competitive election (Garcia vs. Humala runoff).
First round April, runoff June 2006 — peaceful, credible electoral outcome.
Garcia took office July 2006. Congress representative. OAS and international
community accepted outcome. Institutional functioning solid by regional standards.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-2007; OAS election observation

2007.assessment:
Institutions functional. Garcia government implementing market reforms.
Congress competitive (Garcia's APRA party minority government). Judiciary
processing Fujimori extradition case (extradited from Chile September 2007).
Constitutional order maintained. Some corruption issues but not systemic
institutional failure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-2008

2008.assessment:
Functional institutions. Garcia government implementing FTA with US (signed 2006,
implementing 2009). Fujimori trial underway. Congress operating with legislative
output. Bagua tensions building but institutions not threatened. Oil scandal
(Petrogate) rocked Garcia government but resigned minister — institutional
accountability functioning.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-2009

2009.assessment:
Baguazo (June 2009) tested institutional functioning — response was state violence
against indigenous protesters. Fujimori trial continuing — Supreme Court convicted
him (April 2009) to 25 years — landmark institutional moment demonstrating
judicial accountability. Congress independent. US FTA entered force February 2009.
Institutions functional despite political tensions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-2010; Fujimori conviction April 2009

2010.assessment:
Functional institutions. Regional elections (November 2010) held credibly.
Garcia government completing term with declining approval but constitutional
order maintained. Judiciary processing Fujimori era crimes. Congress functioning.
Media free. Electoral institutions prepared for 2011 election.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-2011

2011.assessment:
Competitive elections. Humala won (July 2011). International concern about
Humala's leftist platform but institutions held — he governed more centrist than
feared. Peaceful transition. Congress competitive. Constitutional order maintained.
Keiko Fujimori's Fuerza Popular significant in Congress. Institutions functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
Functional institutions. Humala government facing mining conflicts and social
protests. Congress functioning. Judiciary independent if slow. Constitutional
Tribunal active. State of emergency in some regions for protests — constitutional
power used appropriately. Media free. Electoral institutions functioning.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-2013

2013.assessment:
Institutions functional. Humala managing political tensions. Congress producing
legislation. Judiciary active on corruption cases. Odebrecht investigation
beginning to emerge. Anti-corruption institutions (Contraloria General) functioning.
Some corruption in public procurement. Institutions broadly functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-2014

2014.assessment:
Institutions functioning in slowing economy. Odebrecht beginning to unravel.
Some Cabinet reshuffles reflecting political maneuvering. Congress functioning.
Judiciary active. Regional elections held credibly. 2016 election approaching.
Institutional framework broadly maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014-2015

2015.assessment:
Functional institutions. Odebrecht scandal emerging but not yet full political
impact in Peru. Electoral preparations for 2016 (PPK vs. Keiko Fujimori).
Congress functioning. Judiciary processing cases. Constitutional order maintained.
Media free and competitive.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-2016

2016.assessment:
Institutions functional but entering period of intense political conflict.
PPK won narrow election over Keiko Fujimori (June 2016). Fuerza Popular
controlled Congress with majority. Institutional confrontation between executive
and Congress began immediately. Odebrecht investigations expanding. Institutions
functioning but adversarially.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016-2017

2017.assessment:
Institutions under stress. Congress used interpellation powers aggressively.
Odebrecht scandal expanded to PPK, Kuczynski personally implicated.
First impeachment attempt against PPK (December 2017) survived narrowly
(Keiko Fujimori pardoned Fujimori in exchange for votes — Keiko crisis).
Fujimori pardoned by PPK (December 2017) — immediate constitutional crisis.
Institutions stressed but holding.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017-2018

2018.assessment:
Institutions severely stressed. Constitutional Court overturned Fujimori pardon
(Oct 2018). PPK resigned March 2018 amid second impeachment (Odebrecht bribes
revealed). Vizcarra became president. Vizcarra pursued anti-corruption agenda
— constitutional referendum passed (December 2018) reforming judiciary.
Institutions under stress but constitutional transitions handled by institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018-2019

2019.assessment:
Institutional confrontation peak under Vizcarra. Congress refused confidence
to multiple Cabinets (attempting to trigger dissolution). Vizcarra invoked
constitutional dissolution clause (Article 134) — dissolved Congress September
2019. Constitutional Tribunal authorized (April 2020). New Congress elected
January 2020. Institutions stressed but constitutional mechanisms invoked properly.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019-2020

2020.assessment:
Acute institutional crisis. Congress impeached Vizcarra (November 2020) on
"moral incapacity" charges. Merino installed — mass protests, 2 killed. Congress
elected Sagasti. Three presidents in one week. COVID devastation simultaneous.
Despite crisis, constitutional mechanisms broadly followed though stretched.
Institutions survived but severely tested. Supreme Court upheld constitutional
order.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020-2021

2021.assessment:
Institutions highly strained. Castillo's disputed election (Fujimori allegations)
— JNE (electoral tribunal) validated Castillo after months of challenges.
Castillo took office July 2021. Immediately facing Fujimorista Congress hostile.
Multiple Cabinet reshuffles (5 cabinets in 16 months). Castillo accused of
corruption. Institutional confrontation extreme but constitutional order maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021-2022

2022.assessment:
Severe institutional crisis. Castillo attempted auto-coup (December 7, 2022)
— announced dissolution of Congress unconstitutionally. Congress voted 101-0
to remove him for "moral incapacity." He was arrested. Boluarte (VP) became
president within hours. This was the most serious institutional breakdown since
2000. Subsequent suppression of protests killed 60+. Institutions bent but
ultimately held through removal mechanisms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-2023; Constitutional crisis analysis

2023.assessment:
Institutions stabilizing under Boluarte but severely weakened. Boluarte facing
Congress investigations. Judiciary processing Castillo case. Attorney General
and Constitutional Tribunal active. Congress seeking to extend its term and
delay elections — anti-democratic moves. Press free but threatened.
Institutions partially functional in damaged post-crisis state.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-2024

2024.assessment:
Partial institutional functioning. Boluarte government with low approval.
Congress under multiple corruption investigations. Judiciary processing cases.
2026 election preparation. Congress extended its own term (constitutional question).
Institutions weakened but functional. Odebrecht cases ongoing. Data partial.

2025.assessment:
Institutional situation evolving. 2026 election campaign beginning. Boluarte
government facing continued pressure. Multiple institutional actors operating
with varying independence. Partial data available for 2025.

1995.assessment:
Significant decline from civil war peak. Sendero Luminoso was severely weakened
after Abimael Guzman's 1992 capture. Remaining SL factions in VRAEM still active
but at low level. MRTA declining. Estimated 100-300 conflict-related deaths in 1995.
CVR documented declining trend from ~3,000/year peak in 1984-1990 to hundreds by
1995. SL still conducting attacks but at reduced level. VRAEM main conflict zone.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset; CVR Final Report (Comision de la Verdad y Reconciliacion) 2003

1996.assessment:
Continuing decline in political violence. SL significantly reduced. MRTA Japanese
Embassy seizure (December 1996) spectacular but not reflecting broad military capacity.
Estimated 100-200 conflict deaths in 1996. VRAEM continuing but at reduced level.
Human rights abuses continuing under Fujimori military but at reduced scale from
civil war peak.

sources.citation: UCDP; CVR Final Report 2003; APRODEH (human rights organization)

1997.assessment:
MRTA eliminated as military force after Japanese Embassy rescue (April 1997 —
14 MRTA killed). SL further reduced. Estimated 50-150 conflict deaths. SL remnants
in VRAEM conducting narco-operations rather than political violence. Significant
decline from 1995 level. Transition toward minimal political violence.

1998.assessment:
Very low political violence. SL minimal in VRAEM. MRTA inactive. Estimated
40-80 conflict deaths. Some continued human rights abuses by security forces but
well below earlier period. Country transitioning to post-conflict normality
in most regions. Sporadic SL attacks in VRAEM on military patrols.

1999.assessment:
Minimal conflict deaths. SL reduced to narco-criminal enterprise in VRAEM.
MRTA inactive. Country peaceful in all major urban centers and most rural areas.
Estimated 30-60 deaths from conflict. VRAEM operations occasional. Political
violence replaced by electoral/political crisis (contested 2000 election approach).

2000.assessment:
Very low political violence. Fujimori's fall (September-November 2000) — political
crisis but not violent conflict. Some protesters killed during post-election
demonstrations in Lima but counted as state violence, not insurgency.
SL minimal in VRAEM. Democratic transition occurred peacefully. Estimated fewer
than 50 conflict deaths.

2001.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Democratic restoration under Toledo. SL VRAEM
presence minimal. No organized political violence groups. Country at peace
in political terms — ongoing CVR process documenting past abuses. Post-conflict
period well established. Estimated fewer than 30 deaths.

2002.assessment:
Very low political violence. SL minimal. Arequipazo protests (June 2002)
over privatization — some violence, 2 deaths — more social unrest than
political conflict. UCDP recorded minimal battle deaths. Country essentially
at peace with only residual VRAEM operations.

2003.assessment:
Minimal political violence. SL VRAEM operations very limited. CVR Final Report
presented (August 2003) — documented historical violence, country processing
past. No active armed insurgency of note. Social protests (mining, land disputes)
occasional but not political violence at conflict scale. Country essentially peaceful.

2004.assessment:
Very low political violence. Some SL activity in VRAEM. Social protests
in mining communities occasionally violent but not at conflict scale.
UCDP records minimal deaths. Country at peace. Some Coca-related violence
in VRAEM but primarily criminal in nature.

2005.assessment:
SL VRAEM attack on gas pipeline (June 2005). Andahuianazo (January 2005) led by
Antauro Humala seized police station — killed 4 police. Brief but notable event.
Overall political violence still minimal at national level. UCDP records
low deaths. Country otherwise peaceful.

2006.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Garcia's election peaceful. SL VRAEM
operations continuing at low level. Social conflicts (mining communities)
occasional. Country at peace at national level. UCDP records very few
battle deaths. Post-conflict stability consolidated.

2007.assessment:
Low political violence. Some SL VRAEM attacks. Some police killed in mining
conflicts. Overall minimal. Social protest violence (Bagua tensions building)
not yet reaching conflict scale. Ica earthquake — natural disaster not political
violence. Country essentially at peace.

2008.assessment:
Modest increase in political violence as Bagua tensions rising and SL VRAEM growing.
Mining conflicts in Puno, Cajamarca becoming more violent. Social protest deaths
increasing. UCDP records moderate increase. Still well below conflict scale but
notable uptick from mid-2000s lows.

2009.assessment:
Baguazo (June 2009) — worst political violence event since Fujimori era. Clashes
between indigenous protesters and police in Amazonian region killed 24 police and
approximately 10 civilians (official); higher estimates suggest 30-50 total.
SL VRAEM increasing attacks. Total conflict deaths 60-100. Still low by historical
standards but significant increase. State of emergency declared in Amazonas region.

sources.citation: UCDP; Human Rights Watch, Peru: Baguazo, 2009; UCDP PRIO

2010.assessment:
SL VRAEM attacks increasing — August 2010 ambush killed 10 military.
April 2010 attack killed 8. Mining conflict social protests continuing.
Estimated 40-80 conflict deaths. SL reviving under narco-trafficking revenue
in VRAEM. UCDP records increasing battle deaths from SL vs armed forces.

2011.assessment:
SL VRAEM attacks continuing at elevated level. Mining conflicts in Cajamarca
(Conga) resulting in some deaths. Election year (Humala) without significant
electoral violence. UCDP records moderate battle deaths from SL operations.
Still low by historical standards and by regional comparison.

2012.assessment:
April 2012 SL ambush killed 8 soldiers in VRAEM — one of largest single attacks.
Conga mining conflict (June 2012) emergency zones. Some demonstrator deaths.
UCDP records moderate battle deaths. Political violence at low/minimal level
nationally but VRAEM operations significant. Humala declared state of emergency
in VRAEM.

2013.assessment:
SL leader "Comrade Jose" arrested — significant blow. VRAEM SL operations
reduced after leadership capture. Mining conflict deaths. Overall political
violence at minimal level. UCDP battle deaths declining from 2012 peak.

2014.assessment:
SL VRAEM reduced following leadership capture. Declining political violence.
Some mining conflict deaths. Overall minimal conflict deaths. Country
experiencing political crisis (congressional battles) but not violent conflict.
UCDP records few battle deaths.

2015.assessment:
Very low political violence. SL VRAEM minimal operations. Social protests
in mining areas occasional with few fatalities. Country at peace with
highly competitive electoral politics for 2016. UCDP records very few battle deaths.

2016.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Highly competitive election (PPK vs. Keiko Fujimori)
without violence. SL VRAEM minimal. Social protests at low level. Political
crisis (Fujimorismo vs Kuczynski) beginning but elite-level not violent.
Country at peace. UCDP records few deaths.

2017.assessment:
Very low political violence. Political crisis (Fujimori-PPK congressional battles)
intense but not violent. Nino Costero floods caused deaths but as natural disaster.
SL VRAEM minimal. Social protests in mining areas. Country essentially at peace.

2018.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Kuczynski resignation peaceful. Vizcarra president.
SL VRAEM minimal. Political crisis at executive/congressional level. Social
protests some deaths. UCDP records very few battle deaths. Country at peace.

2019.assessment:
Very low political violence. Congress dissolution constitutional crisis resolved
peacefully. SL VRAEM minimal. Some social protest deaths. Mining conflicts.
Political instability at elite level without violent manifestation. UCDP
records very few battle deaths.

2020.assessment:
Very low political violence. Three presidents in one week (November 2020)
— Merino protests killed 2 demonstrators but quickly resolved. COVID caused
massive mortality but as pandemic, not political violence. SL VRAEM minimal.
UCDP records few battle deaths. Country at peace despite political turmoil.

2021.assessment:
Low political violence. Disputed Castillo election — mass protests but relatively
few deaths. Some mining conflict deaths. Political polarization extreme but
not generating mass violence. SL VRAEM minimal. Castillo government survived
multiple impeachment attempts without violence.

2022.assessment:
Significant increase. Castillo attempted self-coup December 2022; his removal
triggered nationwide protests, particularly intense in Andean south (Puno,
Arequipa, Apurimac, Cusco). Security forces used lethal force against protesters.
60+ civilians killed by security forces in December 2022-January 2023 protests.
This is the most significant political violence since 2009. HRW documented
disproportionate use of force.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Lethal Response: Peru Security Forces and Protesters, 2023

2023.assessment:
Sharp decline from 2022-early 2023 protest peak. Boluarte government maintained
power. Protests continued at lower intensity. Some sporadic mine-related
conflict deaths. SL VRAEM minimal. Political crisis continuing at elite
level without mass violence.

2024.assessment:
Low political violence. Boluarte government continuing. Some social protest
deaths from mining conflicts. SL VRAEM minimal. Political crisis at elite level
continuing but not generating mass violence. 2026 election approaching.

2025.assessment:
Political violence expected to remain minimal. Election year (2026) approaching
with Boluarte facing low approval. Some social protests likely. No organized
armed insurgency of note. SL VRAEM residual. Data incomplete.

1995.assessment:
Peruvian government maintained broad territorial control. Sendero Luminoso (SL)
insurgency was largely defeated by 1993-1995 following capture of Abimael Guzman
in 1992. SL retained minimal presence in VRAEM (Valley of the Apurimac, Ene, and
Mantaro Rivers) coca-growing region. MRTA (Tupac Amaru) also significantly weakened.
Fujimori government controlled virtually all urban and administrative territory.
Emergency zones reduced from prior years.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset; Human Rights Watch Peru reports 1995

1996.assessment:
Government control broadly maintained. SL fractured — Guzman from prison calling
for peace talks, while hardline faction under "Feliciano" continued in VRAEM.
MRTA hostage crisis (December 1996 Japanese Embassy seizure) demonstrated urban
capacity but not territorial control. Government maintained control of all major
cities, departments, and most rural areas.

1997.assessment:
Government territorial control near complete. MRTA hostage crisis resolved April 1997
(military rescue operation — all 14 MRTA hostage-takers killed). SL remnants in VRAEM
reduced to small criminal/narco enterprise. Fujimori government controlled all
significant territory. Emergency zones in VRAEM but small. No major territorial challenge.

1998.assessment:
Fujimori government in full control. SL reduced to criminal narco-trafficking
organization in VRAEM with minimal political-territorial presence. No significant
territorial challenge. Government emergency zones maintained in some Amazonian and
highland areas but as administrative designation rather than reflecting loss of control.

1999.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. SL presence in VRAEM minimal. Fujimori announced
candidacy for controversial third term. No territorial challenges. El Nino 1998
caused humanitarian displacement in north but not territorial control loss. Government
administered all departments and municipalities.

2000.assessment:
Fujimori re-election (May 2000) disputed; OAS and opposition rejected outcome.
Vladivideo scandal (September 2000) showing Montesinos bribing politicians.
Fujimori fled to Japan November 2000 and resigned. Brief political vacuum managed
by interim President Paniagua. Territorial control maintained throughout — political
crisis at elite level, not territorial. No insurgent challenges.

sources.citation: UCDP; Human Rights Watch Peru 2000; ICG Peru reports

2001.assessment:
Democratic restoration under Toledo (elected April 2001, took office July 2001).
Territorial control maintained. No insurgency. SL presence in VRAEM minimal.
Paniagua interim government managed peaceful transition. All departments under
government administration. New democratic legitimacy strengthened territorial
governance.

2002.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Decentralization reforms underway (regional
governments established 2003). Some social protests (Arequipazo, June 2002)
over privatizations — social unrest but not territorial challenge. SL in VRAEM
minimal. Government functioning in all departments.

2003.assessment:
Regional governments established as part of decentralization. Territorial
administration expanding to sub-national level. Some isolated SL attacks in
VRAEM but no territorial control. Toledo government managing social protests
(teachers, miners) but maintaining territorial control. Truth and Reconciliation
Commission (CVR) Final Report presented August 2003.

2004.assessment:
Stable territorial control. Some SL activity in VRAEM. Social protests continuing
(mining conflicts in Puno, Cajamarca) but not territorial challenges. Government
administration functioning across all regions. Security forces active in
coca-producing VRAEM region.

2005.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. SL attack on gas pipeline in VRAEM (June 2005)
showed continuing minimal presence. "Andahuianazo" (January 2005) — brief
regional uprising led by Antauro Humala (Ollanta's brother) in Andahuaylazo — quickly
suppressed. No significant territorial loss. Pre-election period stable.

2006.assessment:
Garcia won election (June 2006, second term). SL in VRAEM continuing limited
activity. Social protests (mining conflicts) common but not territorial challenges.
Government administered all regions. VRAEM coca/SL operations a security challenge
but not territorial control issue at national level.

2007.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. SL VRAEM operations limited. Some social conflicts
around mining and gas extraction projects (Bagua). Ica earthquake (August 2007)
caused humanitarian crisis but not territorial control loss. Government functioning.

2008.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. "Baguazo" tensions building over Amazon indigenous
rights (peaked June 2009). SL VRAEM activity growing with narco ties — some
attacks on military. Growing conflict in extractive industry areas. Overall
government territorial control maintained but VRAEM a continuing security challenge.

2009.assessment:
"Baguazo" (June 5, 2009) — violent clashes between indigenous protesters and
police in Amazonian region over resource extraction decrees, killing 24 police
and 10+ civilians. Temporary loss of government control in Amazonian corridor
near Bagua. Decrees repealed; situation resolved but revealing tensions over
resource extraction. SL VRAEM growing — ambushes on military convoys.

sources.citation: UCDP; Human Rights Watch, Peru: Crisis in the Amazon, 2009

2010.assessment:
Territorial control broadly maintained. SL attacks in VRAEM increasing —
August 2010 SL ambush killed 10 soldiers. VRAEM increasingly a semi-autonomous
zone with narco-SL presence. Garcia government declared emergency in VRAEM.
All other regions under government control. Mining conflicts ongoing.

2011.assessment:
Ollanta Humala won presidential election (June 2011). Territorial control
maintained. SL VRAEM presence continuing. Mining conflicts (Conga, Cajamarca)
generating social unrest. No territorial challenges beyond VRAEM. Government
administered all departments. Humala took more centrist approach than campaigned.

2012.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. VRAEM declared priority zone with military operations
against SL. Mining conflict at Conga gold mine (Cajamarca) led to state of emergency
in 4 regions (June 2012) — major social conflict but temporary. SL attacks in VRAEM.
Government administered all regions normally outside emergency periods.

2013.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. SL in VRAEM continued attacks. April 2012
SL killed 8 soldiers in VRAEM — largest single attack in years. Government
maintained VRAEM operations. Some informal mining and narco-trafficking areas
difficult to govern but not under insurgent control. National territory
administered normally.

2014.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Growth slowdown but no political/territorial crisis.
SL VRAEM presence continuing. Some mining conflicts in Puno and Apurimac. Humala
government facing declining popularity but no territorial challenges. VRAEM narco-
SL operations contained to specific valleys.

2015.assessment:
Territorial control maintained across all departments. SL VRAEM operations
reduced — leader "Comrade Jose" captured 2013, operation dismantling structure.
Some informal mining operations in remote areas outside government reach but not
insurgency. Government administered normally. Economic slowdown not affecting
territorial control.

2016.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Kuczynski won presidential election (June 2016).
SL VRAEM remaining minimal. No territorial challenges. Beginning of period of
intense political instability at congressional/executive level but this was elite-
level conflict, not territorial challenge. All regions governed normally.

2017.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Political crisis: Fujimoristas controlling Congress
using interpellation powers against Kuczynski. Fujimori-PPK confrontation political
crisis but not territorial. Coastal floods (Nino Costero, March 2017) major
humanitarian disaster but not territorial control issue. SL VRAEM minimal.

2018.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Kuczynski resigned March 2018 amid impeachment.
Vizcarra became president. Political crisis intense but institutional — Congress
vs Executive battles. SL VRAEM minimal. All territorial administration continuing.
Odebrecht corruption scandal enveloping multiple presidents but not causing
territorial instability.

2019.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Vizcarra dissolved Congress (September 2019)
— constitutional crisis resolved through new congressional elections January 2020.
All territory administered. SL VRAEM minimal presence. Political instability
at executive-legislative level without territorial manifestation.

2020.assessment:
Territorial control maintained amid severe COVID crisis. Peru had one of world's
highest per capita COVID death rates. Congress impeached Vizcarra (November 2020)
and installed Merino — massive protests forced Merino to resign after 6 days.
Sagasti became president. Three presidents in one week — but no territorial
challenges. Strict lockdown maintained government territorial presence.

sources.citation: UCDP; Human Rights Watch Peru 2020; constitutional crisis reporting

2021.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Castillo (leftist rural teacher) narrowly won
election (July 2021) after disputed count — intense political polarization. Castillo
government immediately under pressure from Fujimorista Congress. SL VRAEM minimal.
Territorial administration continuing. Political crisis at executive level.

2022.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Castillo attempted self-coup (December 7, 2022)
— announced dissolution of Congress and emergency government; Congress voted
to remove him within hours; he was arrested. Boluarte became president.
Nationwide protests erupted — particularly in Andean south — with demonstrators
blocking roads and airports. Some regions (Puno, Apurimac) briefly ungovernable
during protests. Security forces killed 60+ protesters.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Lethal Response: Peru's Security Forces, 2023

2023.assessment:
Territorial control restored after 2022-early 2023 protests. Boluarte government
faced continued low approval but no further major territorial challenges. Some
protests continuing in southern highlands but at lower intensity. Road blockades
periodic. SL VRAEM minimal. Informal mining and coca cultivation areas difficult
to govern but not under insurgent control.

2024.assessment:
Territorial control maintained. Boluarte government continuing with unpopular
rule. Mining conflicts and social protests periodic but not threatening territorial
control. VRAEM coca-SL presence minimal. Some illegal mining areas in Amazon
quasi-ungovernable. Political crisis at elite level with Congress investigations
of Boluarte.

2025.assessment:
Territorial control maintained with 2026 elections approaching. Boluarte
government managing political survival. Social protests continuing in some
regions. VRAEM minimal SL presence. No major new territorial challenges.
Data for 2025 incomplete.

1995.assessment:
Budget transparency minimal under Fujimori. Budget documents not publicly
released in accessible form. Executive budget preparation opaque; Congress
role limited under Fujimori's dominant executive. Off-budget spending through
SIN and military unclear. IBP survey not yet existed; estimated very low
by modern standards. Some fiscal reporting to IFIs (IMF) but limited
public disclosure.

sources.citation: Estimate based on Fujimori-era governance records; pre-IBP era

1996.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Continued Fujimori-era opacity. Budget
information not systematically published. Congressional role in budget
limited. Some improvement over 1990 hyperinflation era in having a
functioning budget process, but public accessibility minimal.

1997.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Fujimori era. Some structural adjustment
transparency requirements from IFIs. Limited public fiscal information.

1998.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. Some improvements in fiscal reporting
required by IMF program. El Nino emergency spending opaque. Overall
limited public disclosure.

1999.assessment:
Minimal-to-limited budget transparency. Political crisis year; budget
process affected by Fujimori third-term preparations. Some fiscal
transparency for IFI compliance.

2000.assessment:
Limited budget transparency. Transitional government (Paniagua) began
improving fiscal disclosure. Toledo-era reforms coming. Paniagua committed
to democratic transparency standards. Some improvement over Fujimori era.

sources.citation: Estimate pre-IBP era; Paniagua transitional reform assessment

2001.assessment:
Limited but improving budget transparency. Toledo government launched
fiscal reform agenda. Transparency Law enacted (2002 preparations). Budget
documents beginning to be published online. SIAF (Integrated Financial
Management System) improving fiscal tracking.

sources.citation: Estimate based on Toledo government fiscal reform trajectory

2002.assessment:
Limited budget transparency transitioning to limited. Transparency Law
(Law 27806) enacted 2002 — major milestone requiring public access to
government information. Budget publication requirements improved. Some
pre-election transparency improvements.

2003.assessment:
Limited budget transparency. Transparency Law implementation ongoing.
Budget documents more accessible. SIAF consolidating. MEF (Ministry of
Finance) publishing more data. Pre-IBP improvements hard to quantify precisely.

sources.citation: Estimate based on Transparency Law implementation

2004.assessment:
Limited-to-substantial budget transparency. Peru Fiscal Responsibility and
Transparency Law strengthened. Budget portal improvements. SIAF data
increasingly accessible. Approaching first IBP survey levels.

sources.citation: Estimate based on pre-IBP reform trajectory

2005.assessment:
Limited-to-substantial budget transparency. Strong improvement trajectory
under Toledo's fiscal reform program. MEF transparency portal active.
Pre-election year; fiscal disclosure high. First IBP survey (2006) would
give 62, suggesting late 2005 close to 58-60.

sources.citation: Estimated pre-IBP; 2006 IBP score was 62 suggesting 2005 near 58-60

2006.assessment:
OBS 2006: score 62 (substantial transparency). First IBP survey for Peru.
Result showed significant budget transparency already established. Transparency
Law 2002 well-implemented. Garcia second term began; continued commitment.
Above median for LAC region. Documents published: Executive Budget Proposal,
Enacted Budget, Mid-Year Review, Year-End Report available.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2006

2007.assessment:
Estimated ~64 (interpolation between 2006: 62 and 2008: 67). Continuing
improvement. Garcia government maintaining and improving fiscal transparency.
SIAF portal expanding. Budget process well-documented.

2008.assessment:
OBS 2008: score 67 (substantial). Improvement from 2006. Peru among best
in LAC for budget transparency. Garcia government continued reforms.
Mid-Year Review published; Audit Report available. Strong documentary trail.
Citizen Budget not published (limitation).

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment:
Estimated ~67 (stable between 2008 and 2010). Garcia government maintaining
transparency standards. SIAF portal. Crisis response fiscal spending tracked.

2010.assessment:
OBS 2010: score 66 (substantial). Stable high performance. MEF website
improvements. Transparency Law consistently applied. Peru among LAC leaders
in budget transparency. Slight dip from 2008 possibly from measurement
methodology changes.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2010

2011.assessment:
Estimated ~65. Humala inauguration; maintained budget transparency systems.
No regression. SIAF maintained. MEF portal active.

2012.assessment:
OBS 2012: score 64 (substantial). Slight decline but still substantial.
Methodology changes may explain some variation. Peru performing solidly.
Budget documents accessible; legislative oversight moderate.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2012

2013.assessment:
Estimated ~64 (stable). Humala government maintaining budget transparency.
No significant changes noted. Consistent substantial disclosure.

2014.assessment:
Estimated ~66. Improving slightly. MEF portal enhancements. Budget documents
consistently published.

2015.assessment:
OBS 2015: score 68 (substantial). Improved. Peru continuing to lead in LAC
budget transparency. Documents published on schedule; in-year reports available.
Legislative oversight somewhat limited (Congressional weakness). Supreme Audit
Institution (CGR) independent and publishing reports.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2015

2016.assessment:
Estimated ~70 (interpolation). PPK government improving transparency further.
Digital government agenda. Open data portal expanding. Budget portal
enhancements.

2017.assessment:
OBS 2017: score 75 (substantial, near extensive). Best Peru score to date.
PPK/Vizcarra government strong on fiscal transparency. Citizen Budget published.
Open data portal. Performance budgeting expanding. CGR active. Despite
political instability (Odebrecht), fiscal transparency mechanisms robust.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2017

2018.assessment:
Estimated ~74. Vizcarra government maintaining high transparency standards.
Open government portal active.

2019.assessment:
OBS 2019: score 74 (substantial). Slight dip from 2017 but maintained high
performance. Vizcarra government anti-corruption agenda including fiscal
transparency. Political crisis (Congress dissolution) did not affect budget
transparency systems.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2019

2020.assessment:
Estimated ~62 (between 2019: 74 and 2021: 51 steep decline). COVID emergency
procurement opacity; Reactiva Peru rapid deployment reduced documentation
standards. Three-presidents crisis. Emergency fiscal measures often not
following standard transparency procedures.

2021.assessment:
OBS 2021: score 51 (limited). Sharp decline from 74 in 2019 — largest drop
in any OBS cycle for Peru. COVID emergency spending opacity; executive
decree-based spending bypassing normal budget procedures. Castillo election
period. In-year reports less complete. Significant regression from 2017-2019
highs.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2021

2022.assessment:
Estimated ~55. Partial recovery from COVID-era decline. Castillo crisis
(December 2022) added further opacity concerns. Boluarte government committed
to fiscal rules but institutional capacity strained. Partial recovery expected.

sources.citation: Estimate based on IBP 2021 trajectory and Boluarte fiscal commitments

2023.assessment:
OBS 2023: score 65 (substantial). Strong recovery from 2021 low. Boluarte
government restored budget transparency standards. MEF portal improved.
Normal budget cycle resumed. Legislative oversight remains limited (Congress
passing immunity laws). CGR active. Substantial improvement from COVID/crisis
period.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2023

2024.assessment:
Estimated ~65. Stable substantial transparency. MEF portal maintained.
Congressional immunity laws reducing legislative oversight quality. Overall
substantial transparency maintained.

2025.assessment:
Estimated ~64. Pre-election year; fiscal transparency expected stable.
No anticipated major changes to budget disclosure framework. Substantial
transparency maintained.

1995.assessment:
Fujimori first term. Legal framework severely compromised. 1992
auto-coup dissolved Congress and courts; 1993 constitution
reestablished nominal rule of law but judiciary subordinated to
executive. Laws accessible in theory but enforcement arbitrary;
constitutional tribunal defanged. SIN (intelligence service) under
Montesinos operated outside legal constraints. Estimated WJP-equivalent
~0.32 (partial_significant_gaps approaching difficult_arbitrary_enforcement).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1996.assessment:
Fujimori consolidating second term (reelected April 1995).
Constitutional Tribunal partially restored but weakened. April 1996
Congress passed law removing three constitutional tribunal judges who
challenged Fujimori third-term reelection interpretation. Legal system
functioning poorly with selective enforcement. Estimated ~0.31.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996

1997.assessment:
May 1997: Congress impeached three Constitutional Tribunal judges
after they ruled Fujimori's third-term bid unconstitutional. This
left the CT non-functional for years. Judiciary effectively
instrumentalized. Laws technically published but enforcement highly
selective. Lowest point of Fujimori-era legal transparency.
Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.30.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997
Human Rights Watch, World Report 1998 - Peru

1998.assessment:
Constitutional Tribunal remained paralyzed (only 4 of 7 judges
seated, no quorum). Judiciary under SIN surveillance. Some positive
signs: Ecuador-Peru Peace Treaty signed October 1998, normalizing
bilateral legal relations. Domestic legal framework remained weak
with arbitrary enforcement. Estimated ~0.30.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998

1999.assessment:
Peru at nadir of legal transparency under Fujimori. Third-term
campaign pushed through despite constitutional prohibition.
Judiciary effectively a tool of executive; opposition figures
harassed through selective prosecution. National Intelligence
System operated outside legal constraints. Laws published but
framework functionally arbitrary. Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.29.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999 (PR=5, CL=4)
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2000 - Peru

2000.assessment:
Year of regime change. Vladivideo tapes (September 2000) revealed
systematic bribery of judges, journalists, and politicians by SIN
head Vladimiro Montesinos. Legal system exposed as comprehensively
corrupted. Fujimori fled to Japan November 2000; Congress declared
him morally unfit. Valentin Paniagua installed as transitional
president. Began process of rebuilding legal institutions.
Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.28 (lowest point).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000 (PR=5, CL=4)
Human Rights Watch, Fujimori's Fall: The Role of Corruption in Peru, 2000

2001.assessment:
Democratic transition under Paniagua (transitional) then Toledo
(elected July 2001). Constitutional Tribunal restored with full
seven judges. Judiciary began reform process. Truth and Reconciliation
Commission established. Legal framework being rebuilt from Fujimori-
era degradation. Significant improvement from 2000 nadir but
structural weaknesses remained. Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.33.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001

2002.assessment:
Toledo government consolidating democratic institutions. Law 27806
(Transparency and Access to Public Information, 2002) enacted —
major milestone establishing FOI framework. Constitutional Tribunal
functioning. TRC investigating human rights abuses. Legal
transparency improving markedly from Fujimori nadir. Estimated ~0.36.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002
Peru Law 27806 - Ley de Transparencia y Acceso a la Información Pública, 2002

2003.assessment:
FOI law (Law 27806, 2002) entering implementation phase. TRC
report nearing completion. Some judicial corruption scandals
continued but systemic Fujimori-era bribery dismantled. Toledo
government had own corruption issues (family scandal). Legal
framework improving but enforcement inconsistent. Estimated ~0.37.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003

2004.assessment:
TRC final report (2003) published, establishing accountability
framework for Fujimori-era abuses. Constitutional Court active.
FOI law in effect but implementation patchy in regional governments.
Judicial independence improving but backlogs severe. Estimated
WJP-equivalent ~0.38 (partial_significant_gaps).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004

2005.assessment:
Toledo final year; legal framework stable but unenforced in many
areas. Constitutional Tribunal handling significant caseload.
2005: Supreme Court began Fujimori extradition proceedings from
Chile (where he had traveled). Legal access framework (FOI) in
place but widely evaded by local governments. Estimated ~0.38.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
Alan Garcia second term (elected June 2006). Garcia had served
first term 1985-1990 ending in hyperinflation/crisis; returned
as reformed centrist. Legal framework relatively stable. FOI law
functioning in central government. Ongoing judicial reform
programs. Fujimori captured in Chile November 2005; extradition
proceedings underway. Estimated ~0.39.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006

2007.assessment:
Fujimori extradited from Chile September 2007 — landmark for
rule of law. High-profile corruption trials underway for Fujimori
and Montesinos. Economic boom supporting institutional capacity.
Judicial reform programs active. FOI framework functioning at
central government level. Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.39.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007

2008.assessment:
Fujimori trial began December 2007 (verdict 2009). Major milestone
for accountability. Garcia government advancing legal framework
reforms including transparency decrees for extractive sector.
Economic boom providing resources for institutional capacity.
Constitutional Tribunal active. Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.40
(approaching most_accessible_some_gaps boundary).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008

2009.assessment:
Fujimori convicted April 2009 — 25 years for crimes against
humanity and corruption. Landmark for rule of law accountability.
However, Baguazo massacre (June 2009) revealed how indigenous
rights and legal protections were systematically ignored in
Amazon region. Executive decree powers (DLs) used aggressively
without congressional approval. Estimated ~0.40.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2010 - Peru (Baguazo)

2010.assessment:
Garcia final year. Legal framework relatively stable at central
government level. Baguazo aftermath: indigenous consultation
rights debate. Constitutional Tribunal issuing significant rulings.
Judiciary reform program ongoing. Pre-consultation law being
debated (enacted 2011). Estimated WJP-equivalent ~0.40; first
actual WJP measurement expected 2011.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010

2011.assessment:
First WJP measurement year. Humala elected June 2011 (nationalist-
left). WJP Factor 3 (Open Government) 0.43 — partial_significant_gaps.
Includes subfactors: publicized laws/government data, right to
information, civic participation, complaint mechanisms. Peru's
score reflects functioning FOI law, active CT, but weak
implementation in subnational governments and extractive sector.
Prior Consultation Law enacted September 2011 (ILO Convention 169).

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2011
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011

2012.assessment:
WJP Factor 3: 0.44. Modest improvement. Humala government
passing significant legislation including Prior Consultation
implementation. Pacific Alliance legal harmonization efforts.
FOI portal (gob.pe) expanding. Constitutional Tribunal active
on social/economic rights. Mining conflict in Cajamarca
(Conga project) highlighted gaps between law and enforcement
in extractive sector. Still partial_significant_gaps.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2012

2013.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.44 (stable). Peru joined Open Government
Partnership (OGP) 2012; first National Action Plan 2012-2014
