commitments on transparency. Open data portal expanding.
Odebrecht construction scandal beginning (though not yet
revealed publicly). Legal framework stable, enforcement gaps
remain particularly in judiciary. Mining and environmental
regulatory compliance weak. Score stable.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2012-2013
Open Government Partnership, Peru National Action Plan 2012-2014

2014.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.45. Humala final year; governance consolidating.
OGP second action plan. Digital government portal improving.
Judiciary reform commission active. However, Humala government
using emergency decrees for security (Ley Pulpín protest Nov-Dec
2014, labor law decree). Legal transparency improving at formal
level but enforcement gaps persist. Approaching most_accessible_some_gaps
boundary (~0.47).

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2014

2015.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.46. Humala concluding presidency. Odebrecht
scandal beginning to surface in Brazil (Car Wash investigation
2014-2015); Peru connections not yet revealed. Open data
portal (datosabiertos.gob.pe) active. OGP commitments on
procurement transparency. Legal framework for access to
information functioning at central government level.
Subnational compliance weak. Still partial_significant_gaps.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2015

2016.assessment:
PPK (Pedro Pablo Kuczynski) elected June 2016 (narrow victory
over Keiko Fujimori). WJP Factor 3 ~0.47 — peak pre-Odebrecht
revelation year. PPK brought technocratic reform agenda including
institutional strengthening. November 2016: Odebrecht plea deal
in US revealed massive bribery of Peruvian officials.
Constitutional crisis beginning. Borderline partial_significant_gaps
/ most_accessible_some_gaps.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2016
Odebrecht plea agreement, US DOJ, December 2016

2017.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.46. Odebrecht scandal fully erupting — three
former presidents (Toledo, García, Humala) implicated. Humala
jailed briefly on money laundering charges. PPK's Odebrecht
connections revealed. Congress (Fujimorista majority under
Keiko) weaponized impeachment. Political crisis significantly
undermined legal predictability and judicial independence
perception. Slight decline from 2016 peak.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2017-2018
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017

2018.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.45. PPK pardoned Fujimori December 2017
(controversial); Congress voted to remove PPK March 2018 for
Odebrecht connections. VP Vizcarra assumed presidency. Supreme
Court justice (CNM) scandal: audio recordings revealed judges
negotiating sentences — "Los Cuellos Blancos del Puerto."
Vizcarra closed Congress September 2019 (upheld by CT).
Judicial credibility severely damaged despite technical legal
framework functioning.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2019
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2019 - Peru

2019.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 0.45. Vizcarra dissolved Congress September 2019
(constitutional closure under Art. 134 — constitutional crisis
debated). New Congress elected January 2020. Ex-president Garcia
committed suicide April 2019 as arrest warrant issued (Odebrecht).
Anti-corruption reforms continuing but judicial system credibility
damaged. Odebrecht cooperation agreements yielding prosecutions.
Stable at partial_significant_gaps level.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2019

2020.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.44. Constitutional crisis: Congress voted to
impeach Vizcarra (morally incapacitated) November 9, 2020.
Congressional speaker Merino became president; massive protests
led to his resignation November 15 after 6 days. Sagasti
appointed transitional president. Three presidents in one week.
COVID emergency reduced transparency in procurement (exception
contracting). Slight decline from 2019.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2020
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021

2021.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.42. Pedro Castillo elected July 2021 (radical
left-wing teacher/union leader). Castillo appointed loyalists
with corruption backgrounds to key positions; frequent cabinet
reshuffles (5 prime ministers in 16 months). Prosecutors
investigating Castillo for corruption from first months.
Judicial independence pressured by executive appointments.
Legal predictability declining. Decline from 2020.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2021
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022

2022.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.41. Castillo under multiple corruption
investigations; Congress impeachment attempts (rejected twice).
December 7, 2022: Castillo attempted self-coup — announced
dissolution of Congress and emergency government by decree;
military and police refused orders; Cabinet resigned en masse;
Congress impeached within hours; Boluarte assumed presidency.
Castillo arrested. 60+ protesters killed in subsequent crackdown.
Rule of law stressed to breaking point.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2022
Human Rights Watch, Peru Events of 2022

2023.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.40. Boluarte government facing mass protests
through January 2023 (Dina regime); state of emergency declared.
Congress passed controversial laws restricting prosecution of
corruption. National Board of Justice (JNJ) under threat from
Congress (impeachment motions against JNJ members). Judiciary
independence under sustained attack. International human rights
bodies criticizing impunity for protest deaths. Significant
erosion of rule of law norms.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2023
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023

2024.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 ~0.39. Congress continued assault on anti-corruption
institutions: laws passed weakening prosecutor independence,
restricting international court jurisdiction, and modifying
judicial appointment processes. Boluarte government facing
own corruption investigations (Rolex scandal). JNJ suspended
by Constitutional Tribunal (later restored). CICIG-equivalent
not created. Peru's rule of law at lowest point since early
2000s democratic transition.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2023-2024
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
Estimated ~0.39 (stable/slight decline). Boluarte continuing
as president through 2025 elections. Congress-executive alliance
continuing to weaken anti-corruption institutions. 2026 elections
approaching with multiple candidates under investigation.
No significant institutional recovery expected. WJP 2025
data not yet published; estimate based on 2024 trend.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2023-2024 (projected)
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025 (preliminary)

1995.assessment:
Very serious press freedom situation under Fujimori. Chicha press receiving
government payments to publish favorable content and discredit opposition.
SIN monitored journalists; some harassed and threatened. Major TV channels
under direct or indirect government control (Montesinos bribed owners).
Independent print media operating under self-censorship. Estimated raw
score ~55 (very serious).

sources.citation: Freedom House Press Freedom Report 1995; CPJ Peru country reports

1996.assessment:
Very serious. Chicha press consolidation. Baruch Ivcher (Frecuencia Latina)
case developing. Montesinos paying media owners. Self-censorship pervasive.

sources.citation: CPJ Peru country reports 1996; Freedom House

1997.assessment:
Very serious. Baruch Ivcher denaturalized (July 1997) for Frecuencia Latina
broadcasting SIN corruption and torture. Canal 2 forcibly transferred to
pro-government shareholders. Constitutional Tribunal dismissed. Press freedom
at Fujimori nadir.

sources.citation: CPJ Peru 1997; OAS IACHR Ivcher case

1998.assessment:
Very serious. Continued media control after Ivcher case. Advertising boycotts
coordinated against independent media. SIN monitoring of journalists documented.
Some independent print operating under self-censorship.

1999.assessment:
Very serious. Third-term election preparations intensified media control.
Most TV channels aligned with Fujimori. International journalists restricted.
OAS observer mission reported unfair media coverage. Peak media repression.

sources.citation: CPJ Peru 1999; OAS Electoral Observation Mission

2000.assessment:
Very serious transitioning to improving. Vladivideo (September 2000) broke;
Montesinos system collapsed. Bribed media reverted to independence. Chicha
press lost government subsidies and collapsed. Ivcher returned to Canal 2.
Year split: very serious H1, rapidly improving H2.

sources.citation: CPJ Peru 2000; Freedom House Press Freedom

2001.assessment:
Difficult. Dramatic improvement under Toledo. Chicha press collapsed. Independent
TV fully operational. Critical media flourishing. Self-censorship largely ended.
Drug trafficking region risks but political restrictions lifted.

2002.assessment:
RSF 2002: raw score ~37 (difficult). First year of RSF index. Peru ~60th
globally — significant improvement from Fujimori era. Press free to investigate.
Some physical risks for journalists in conflict-affected regions.

2003.assessment:
RSF 2003: raw score ~36 (difficult). Stable. Political press freedom maintained.
CVR process covered freely. Some risks from drug traffickers. Functioning
press environment.

2004.assessment:
RSF 2004: raw score ~37 (difficult). Stable. Desacato (insult) laws used
for legal harassment of journalists. Press generally free but colonial-era
press restrictions remaining on books.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2004; CPJ Peru

2005.assessment:
RSF 2005: raw score ~35 (difficult). Stable. Press freedom maintained.
Pre-election competitive media environment. Good political coverage.

2006.assessment:
RSF 2006: raw score ~36 (difficult). Election year with free press coverage.
Garcia elected; initially press free. Organized crime threats in drug regions.

2007.assessment:
RSF 2007: raw score ~38 (difficult). Garcia government using defamation
lawsuits against journalists. Drug trafficking threats in provinces. Overall
functional but problematic areas.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2007; CPJ Peru

2008.assessment:
RSF 2008: raw score ~40 (difficult/problematic boundary). Garcia government
pressure against critical journalists. Amazon indigenous rights reporters
at risk. Petrogate coverage — investigative journalism functioning.

2009.assessment:
RSF 2009: raw score ~42 (problematic). Baguazo: journalists covering Amazon
protests faced restrictions and threats. Garcia government narrative control.
CPJ documented journalist harassment.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2009; CPJ Peru

2010.assessment:
RSF 2010: raw score ~40 (difficult). Slight improvement. Pre-election free
media environment. Political coverage competitive. Drug trafficking threats
in provinces ongoing.

2011.assessment:
RSF 2011: raw score ~40 (difficult). Humala elected; media initially concerned.
Humala moderated; press remained free. Mining conflict zone journalists
at risk. Overall stable difficult environment.

2012.assessment:
RSF 2012: raw score ~40 (difficult). Stable. Conga conflict: journalists
harassed. Defamation cases. Media concentration concerns.

2013.assessment:
RSF 2013: raw score ~39 (difficult). RSF new methodology. Peru stable in
difficult classification. Investigative journalism active (Odebrecht beginning).
Drug trafficking threats in provinces.

2014.assessment:
RSF 2014: raw score ~41 (difficult). CPJ documented journalist impunity
(killings uninvestigated). VRAEM journalists at risk. Media concentrated.
Some legal harassment continuing.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2014; CPJ Peru

2015.assessment:
RSF 2015: raw score ~41 (difficult). Some journalists murdered in drug
trafficking regions (uninvestigated). Political coverage free during
pre-election. Odebrecht investigative journalism expanding.

2016.assessment:
RSF 2016: raw score ~39 (difficult). Election year with free press coverage.
PPK elected; Odebrecht investigative journalism accelerating. Strong critical
media environment.

2017.assessment:
RSF 2017: raw score ~40 (difficult). PPK-Fujimorismo conflict driving intense
media coverage. Odebrecht journalism prominent. Some politician harassment
of journalists. Overall functional.

2018.assessment:
RSF 2018: raw score ~38 (difficult). Vizcarra government generally supportive
of press freedom. Anti-corruption investigations created positive environment.
Media ownership concentration concerns ongoing.

2019.assessment:
RSF 2019: raw score ~37 (difficult). Continued improvement. Congress dissolution
covered freely. Anti-corruption coverage strong. Regions with organized crime
risks. Overall functioning.

2020.assessment:
RSF 2020: raw score ~37 (difficult). Three-presidents crisis covered freely.
COVID restrictions limited some journalist access. Protest crackdown (Inti and
Bryan killed November) covered by independent media. Sagasti government
not restricting press.

2021.assessment:
RSF 2021: raw score ~38 (difficult). Castillo election period. Press free
to cover disputed election. Some Castillo supporters hostile to Lima media.
Organized crime threats in provinces ongoing.

2022.assessment:
RSF 2022: raw score ~44 (problematic). Worsening. Castillo government hostile
to critical Lima media. Protest crackdown (December 2022) limited media
access in southern highlands. Boluarte's use of security forces limiting
coverage.

2023.assessment:
RSF 2023: raw score ~45 (problematic). Congressional legislation limiting
public information access. Investigative journalists facing legal harassment.
Boluarte restricting information about protest deaths.

2024.assessment:
RSF 2024: raw score ~45 (problematic). Stable at worsened level. Congressional
immunity laws reducing investigative targets. Journalist harassment. Rolexgate
coverage shows investigative journalism functioning but under increasing
pressure.

2025.assessment:
RSF 2025: estimated ~45 raw (problematic). Pre-election year; some improvement
in political coverage expected. Congressional transparency restrictions
concerning. Baseline decline from 2016-2019 levels maintained.

1995.assessment:
Moderate statistical transparency. INEI (Instituto Nacional de Estadistica e
Informatica) functional but with limited resources. National census 1993 completed.
Economic data produced but with delays. Poverty data limited. Under Fujimori,
some statistical manipulation alleged in reporting economic progress. WB SCI
series begins 2004; estimated ~55 for 1995 based on institutional capacity
at the time.

sources.citation: INEI institutional history; World Bank Peru statistical assessment

1996.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. INEI publishing national accounts, inflation
data (CPI), trade statistics. Some improvements with Fujimori economic program
IFI requirements for data. Population data from 1993 census.

1997.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. Growing with economic boom. National accounts
improving. Continuous Household Survey (ENAHO) beginning. Trade and balance
of payments data quality improving.

1998.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity. El Nino affected some data collection.
INEI capacity building continuing with World Bank support. Poverty statistics
improving.

1999.assessment:
Moderate-to-good statistical capacity. INEI improving. National accounts,
CPI, trade, labor force data produced regularly. Some delays and revisions.
Approaching good capacity level.

2000.assessment:
Good statistical capacity emerging. Transitional government improved
transparency requirements for statistical agencies. INEI independence from
political interference improving. Toledo government would further strengthen.

2001.assessment:
Good statistical capacity. Toledo government strengthened INEI institutional
independence. New national census 2007 preparation beginning. ENAHO household
survey producing poverty data. National accounts improved.

2002.assessment:
Good statistical capacity. INEI autonomous. Poverty maps published. ENAHO
annual household survey operational. GDP quarterly data available. Regional
statistics improving with decentralization.

2003.assessment:
Good statistical capacity. CVR using government statistics; INEI data
credible. Improving data sharing with international community. Approaching
2004 SCI baseline.

2004.assessment:
WB SCI 2004: ~68 (good). First year of SCI data for Peru. INEI well-
established institution. Regular national accounts, CPI, trade, labor force
survey, poverty survey. Methodology generally sound. 2007 national census
preparation underway.

2005.assessment:
WB SCI 2005: ~69 (good). Slight improvement. National accounts base year
revision. ENAHO providing annual poverty data. INEI website improving data
dissemination.

2006.assessment:
WB SCI 2006: ~70 (good). Garcia second term maintaining INEI quality.
Quarterly GDP estimates improving timeliness. Labor force survey (ENAHO)
annual. Business survey improving.

2007.assessment:
WB SCI 2007: ~71 (good). National Census 2007 conducted — major statistical
milestone. INEI publishing results improving demographic data accuracy.
Upward trend in statistical capacity.

2008.assessment:
WB SCI 2008: ~72 (good). Census data integrated. National accounts revised
with census-updated population. Strong statistical infrastructure. International
data sharing active. INEI respected regional institution.

2009.assessment:
WB SCI 2009: ~73 (good). Continued improvement. Crisis year data well-tracked.
INEI rapid employment surveys during global financial crisis. Poverty data
reliable. Strong institutional capacity.

2010.assessment:
WB SCI 2010: ~74 (good). Strong statistical capacity. GDP revisions
incorporating new base year. Poverty mapping at subnational level improving.
INEI data internationally cited and used by World Bank, IMF.

2011.assessment:
WB SCI 2011: ~74 (good). Stable. Humala government maintained INEI
independence. Strong data production. Agricultural census preparation.

2012.assessment:
WB SCI 2012: ~75 (good). Near high. Agricultural Census 2012 conducted.
National accounts chain-linking methodology. INEI open data portal improving.
Strong regional comparison.

2013.assessment:
WB SCI 2013: ~76 (good). Strong statistical system. Multiple surveys:
ENAHO, ENDES (DHS), business surveys, monthly industrial production.
Data dissemination improving through INEI portal.

2014.assessment:
WB SCI 2014: ~77 (good/excellent boundary). Peak SCI performance. Strong
across all dimensions: periodicity, source data, methodology. INEI among
best-performing statistical offices in LAC.

2015.assessment:
WB SCI 2015: ~77 (good). Stable at high level. Inter-census population
estimates strong. Quarterly GDP flash estimates. Poverty data robust.

2016.assessment:
WB SCI 2016: ~77 (good). Stable high performance. PPK government digital
government agenda supporting open data. INEI data quality maintained.

2017.assessment:
WB SCI 2017: ~76 (good). Slight dip. El Nino Costero data disruptions.
GDP revision process. Otherwise strong institutional capacity maintained.

2018.assessment:
WB SCI 2018: ~77 (good). Recovery. Vizcarra open government agenda.
INEI data quality maintained. Strong national accounts.

2019.assessment:
WB SCI 2019: ~78 (good). Near peak. Constitutional crisis covered with good
data. Census 2017 data integrated. Poverty estimates robust.

2020.assessment:
WB SCI 2020: ~73 (good). COVID disrupted data collection — household surveys
moved to phone interviews reducing quality. Lockdown limited fieldwork.
Three-presidents crisis. SCI final year (replaced by SPI from 2021).
Still good performance despite disruptions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2020 (final year)

2021.assessment:
SPI equivalent ~72 (good). WB replaced SCI with SPI in 2021 (different
methodology). COVID recovery in data collection. Castillo election data
tracking. INEI still strong institution. SPI not directly comparable to
SCI but estimate suggests continued good capacity.

2022.assessment:
SPI equivalent ~71 (good). INEI continued normal operations despite
Castillo crisis and protest disruptions. Data quality maintained. Quarterly
GDP estimates continued.

2023.assessment:
SPI equivalent ~71 (good). Stable. INEI institutional quality maintained.
Budget constraints affecting some INEI programs. GDP recession tracked
accurately. Boluarte government not interfering with statistics.

2024.assessment:
SPI equivalent ~71 estimated. INEI stable. GDP recovery tracked. Poverty
surveys ongoing. No major changes to statistical capacity.

2025.assessment:
SPI equivalent ~71 projected. INEI expected to maintain institutional quality.
Pre-election year; census preparations for future cycle. Good statistical
transparency maintained.
